#AreaNews |ADVANCING TRANSPARENT YOUTH GOVERNANCE IN THE PROVINCE OF BATAAN DURING THE BAYBAYIN CAMP CAPACITY BUILDING ACTIVITY

The Provincial Government of Bataan, through the Provincial Social Welfare and Development Office (PSWDO) â€“ Local Youth Development Division, under the leadership of the Provincial Governor of Bataan, Hon. Jose Enrique S. Garcia III, and Provincial Youth Development Officer (PYDO) of Bataan, Ms. Joie Dee R. Rubiano, successfully conducted the â€œBaybayin Camp: SK, LYDO and LYDC Capacity Buildingâ€ last February 25 to 27, 2026, at the Pavilion Tourism, Balanga City, Bataan, to strengthen the technical capacity of youth leaders and ensure alignment with the National Youth Commission (NYC)â€™s Philippine Youth Development Plan.

Hon. Lovely Joy A. Poblete, Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) Provincial Pederasyon President, delivered her welcome remarks, expressing gratitude to the National Youth Commission for the technical assistance, while Ms. Rubiano, PYDDO of Bataan, presented the objectives and overview of the activity.

The NYC, through the Pampanga Area Office, facilitated the session â€œRecode & Reboot: Compliance and Updates in Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) and the SK Web Portal (SKWP),â€ which provided a walkthrough of account creation, report submission timelines, and new portal features. Additionally, they discussed the SK Local Planning Guidelines under the Department of the Interior and Local Government-Department of Budget and Management-NYC (DILG-DBM-NYC) Joint Memorandum Circular (MC) No. 1, s. 2025, and SK Monitoring and Evaluation Reporting, pursuant to DILG MC 2024-061, emphasizing policy compliance, planning alignment and transparency. The activityconcluded with an open forum, which strengthened the resolve of the young leaders to promote transparent governance.

Through initiatives like the Baybayin Camp, young leaders are equipped not only with technical knowledge but also with the responsibility to champion transparency, accountability, and meaningful youth participation in governance.

When empowered, the Filipino youth lead the way in building stronger communities and a better nation.

#AreaNews |THE CITY OF SAN JOSE DEL MONTE STRENGTHENS YOUTH PARTICIPATION THROUGH LOCAL YOUTH DEVELOPMENT PLAN WRITESHOP

The City Government of San Jose del Monte, Bulacan, through the City Youth and Sports Development Office led by City Youth Development Officer Mr. Luis R. Gutierrez, conducted the activity â€œFrom Vision to Action: A Youth-Led Writeshop for the Local Youth Development Plan (LYDP) 2026â€“2028.â€

The initiative aimed to engage youth leaders in shaping the cityâ€™s Local Youth Development Plan, ensuring that programs and policies for young people are evidence-based, participatory, and aligned with the Philippine Youth Development Plan (PYDP) 2023â€“2028.

Through the writeshop, youth leaders were provided with guidance on local youth development planning, priority setting, and program design, enabling them to translate the needs and aspirations of San JoseÃ±o youth into concrete strategies and initiatives for the next three years.

The National Youth Commission (NYC), through the Pampanga Area Office, provided technical assistance by presenting the PYDP framework and Local Youth Development Planning guidelines, helping participants identify priority programs under the PYDP Centers of Youth Participation.

This initiative reinforces the City Governmentâ€™s commitment to institutionalizing meaningful youth participation in local governance, ensuring that San JoseÃ±o youth remain active partners in shaping policies and programs that directly impact their future.

Empowering young people to participate in planning today ensures responsive and inclusive youth development tomorrow.

#AreaNews | Y-GOV 2026 TRAINING TO STRENGTHEN THE CAPACITY OF SK PAMPANGA IN HANDLING GOVERNMENT SYSTEMS AND PROCESSES

The Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) Federation of Candaba, in coordination with the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) Candaba Field Office, Commission on Audit and the Candaba Local Youth Development Office (LYDO), successfully conducted the three-day training-seminar, â€œY-GOV 2026: Youth in Governance - Enhancing SK Knowledge on Government Systems and Processesâ€ last February 16 to 18, 2026, at Subic Bay, Zambales. 

The activity aimed to strengthen SK officials' capacity by deepening their understanding of government systems and processes and more importantly, to promote effective, transparent and accountable youth governance. Candabaâ€™s SK Federation President Hon. Luis Gabriel Sagum delivered the welcome remarks, encouraging participants to prepare for SK Web Portal monitoring and evaluation (M&E) submissions and Local Youth Development Plan formulation, while Engr. Khalid F. Sunggod of DILG Candaba presented the activity overview.

The National Youth Commission (NYC), through the Pampanga Area Office, led the session â€œRecode & Reboot: Compliance and Updates in Monitoring and Evaluation and the SK Web Portal,â€ guiding participants on account creation, report submission timelines and portal updates to reinforce transparency. 

Furthermore, they discussed the SK Local Planning Guidelines under the DILG-Department of Budget and Management-NYC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, s. 2025 and SK Monitoring and Evaluation Reporting pursuant to DILG Memorandum Circular 2024-061, emphasizing alignment with planning, policy compliance, and accountability.

#AreaNews | CEBU CITY YOUTH LEADERS STREAMLINE COMPLIANCE AND REPORTING AT 18TH SANGGUNIANG KABATAAN ASSEMBLY

The 18th Sangguniang Kabataan Federation (SK) General Assembly of Cebu City was held last February 15, 2026, at the Cebu City Hall. The meeting was presided by SK Federation President Hon. Rhea Jakosalem. The National Youth Commission (NYC), through the Cebu Area Office, provided updates on the compliance and submission of local youth development requirements, presented an orientation on the SK Web Portal registration and reports encoding process, and discussed the guidelines and procedures for the issuance of the Certificate of Good Standing for SK officials. Members were encouraged to strictly adhere to national guidelines and improve internal monitoring mechanisms within their respective barangays.

Local Youth Development Officer, Mr. Paolo Saberon, underscored the importance of the Annual Barangay Youth Investment Plan (ABYIP) and aligning youth programs with barangay development priorities. He also emphasized the strengthening of youth-centered programs through planning, documentation, and coordination between SK officials and local governments to maximize their effectiveness and impact.

Overall, these presentations gave participants comprehensive knowledge in fulfilling their responsibilities as youth leaders, as these are important in maintaining accurate and timely reporting, credibility, and institutional integrity. After discussing upcoming activities to enhance youth civic engagement, leadership, and participation in community-building, the body reaffirmed its dedication to promoting effective youth governance and participation in forwarding not only local but also national development.

#AreaNews | REGIONAL LEADERS CONVENE IN BAGUIO CITY TO ADVANCE EDUCATION AND WORKFORCE DEVELOPMENT

The 1st Semestral Meeting of the Education and Workforce Development Subcommittee of the Regional Development Council (RDC), under the leadership of the Technical Education and Skills Development Authority (TESDA) Regional Director Mr. Glenn Murphy, with the Department of Education - Cordillera Administrative Region (DepEd-CAR) and TESDA as secretariat, convened its members last February 27, 2026, at the Cordillera State Institute of Technology Education Military Cut-Off, Baguio City. 

The meeting reconstituted its subcommittee to include new members, such as the National Youth Commission (NYC), Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG), Parent-Teacher Association (PTA), Department of Migrant Workers (DMW), Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT), and Dulche Chocolates, to ensure the youth are well-represented in regional education and workforce planning. In addition, a change to gender-neutral nomenclature was adopted.

The NYC, through the Baguio Area Office, served as a resource speaker on the Philippine Youth Development Plan, youth engagement, and strategies to integrate the youth in policy formulation and program implementation. Through this, the NYC underscores the crucial role the youth play in regional developmentâ€“one that is inclusive, sustainable, and responsive to current and future challenges and needs of the people.

In celebration of National Womenâ€™s Month, the National Youth Commission (NYC) reaffirms its commitment to advancing gender equality and the protection of women. Through an Ad Referendum Resolution sponsored by NYC Chairperson and CEO Undersecretary Joseph Francisco â€œJeffâ€ R. Ortega, the Commission expressed its support for the nationwide observance of National Womenâ€™s Month and calls on all Filipino youth to ensure that women are treated with fairness, professionalism, respect and dignity in our communities, institutions and workplaces.

The Commission also encourages Sangguniang Kabataan leaders and youth organizations across the country to create and promote projects that teach their communities, constituents, and stakeholders the importance of respecting women, especially among those who hold positions of leadership and public trust.

Recent negative and objectifying remarks about women circulating in public forums highlight an important truth: how leaders speak about women matters. Public office carries the responsibility to demonstrate integrity, restraint and respect in both words and actions.

Respect for women is non-negotiable. The Filipino youth are watching. The next generation will stand with leaders who uphold equality, integrity and accountability â€” and will remember those who fail to do so.

#AreaNews | THE NATIONAL YOUTH COMMISSION PAYS COURTESY VISIT TO BAGUIO CITY MAYOR BENJAMIN MAGALONG 

The National Youth Commission (NYC), represented by the Commissioner representing Luzon Assistant Secretary Gervy James Gumarit, together with the NYC - Baguio Area Office and Police Provincial Director of Benguet Lt. Col. Lambert Suerte, conducted a courtesy call with Mayor Benjamin Magalong at the City Hall in Baguio City last March 2, 2026.

The meeting served as a strategic dialogue on strengthening the role of the youth in governance and development. The NYC expressed its dedication to take a proactive role in shaping youth consciousness and upholding values centered on good governance, such as accountability, transparency, discipline, integrity, and public service ethics. 

Mayor Magalong shared similar sentiments about the importance of values-based and principle-centered leadership, where the youth must be molded to be both responsible citizens and future leaders rooted in service. Furthermore, he stressed that youth empowerment must go beyond activities and events as it should translate into actual participation in decision making processes, local development planning, and community engagements. 

The courtesy call put emphasis on the importance of inter-agency collaboration to promote peacebuilding, civic responsibility, and social cohesion, as well as building strong partnerships to advance programs that empower the Filipino youth to be socially responsible and service-oriented leaders. This highlighted the youthâ€™s crucial role in being agents of change, capable of contributing to inclusive development, sustainable peace, transformative governance, and beyond.

#AreaNews | â€œSTRENGTHENING LOCAL YOUTH DEVELOPMENT COUNCILS: ITS GUIDELINES AND BEST PRACTICESâ€ WEBINAR ENCOURAGES YOUTH PARTICIPATION IN GOVERNANCE

The National Youth Commission (NYC), through the Naga Area Office and in partnership with the Provincial Youth Development Office of Catanduanes, conducted an online webinar entitled â€œStrengthening Local Youth Development Councils: Its Guidelines and Best Practicesâ€ last February 24, 2026, via Zoom.

To ensure the institutionalization of youth representation in local development structures and enhance the Local Youth Development Officers, Sangguniang Kabataan officials, and youth development stakeholders, the activity provided knowledge on the operational guidelines and best practices needed to sustain the accountability and effectiveness of Local Youth Development Councils.

Mr. Robert D. Perico, the Local Youth Development Officer of Tigaon, Camarines Sur, emphasized the legal framework behind the LYDC, which serves as a mechanism for inclusive and multi-sectoral youth participation in local governance, as per its mandate. He also shared practical applications of policy frameworks when it comes to on-the-ground experiences as scenario examples to provide better guidance. A forum for dialogue and clarification was opened for participants to deepen their understanding of the topics. 

To conclude the event, the NYC-inspired participants were encouraged to continue their active engagement in advancing youth development initiatives and reminded them that maintaining a strengthened LYDC encourages consistent institutional support. Together with LYDCs, Filipino youth leaders can work hand in hand as they proactively lead and engage with their communities toward development.

#AreaNews | THE NATIONAL YOUTH COMMISSION HOLDS â€œREGISTER, RECOGNIZE, REPRESENT: AN ORIENTATION ON THE YOUTH ORGANIZATION REGISTRATION PROGRAMâ€ WEBINAR

To promote active citizenship and meaningful youth participation, the National Youth Commission (NYC), through the Naga Area Office and in partnership with the Provincial Youth Development Office (PYDO) of Camarines Sur, conducted the â€œRegister, Recognize, and Represent: An Orientation on the Youth Organization Registration Program (YORP)â€ Zoom webinar last February 23, 2026.

The webinar aimed to improve the understanding among youth and youth-serving organizations, local government units (LGUs), and youth development stakeholders about the NYCâ€™s YORP, which encourages youth representation and engagement in local governance and development processes.

Opening the program was Mx. Valentine Sabareza from the Camarines Sur PYDO, who emphasized YORP as a vital mechanism for strengthening legitimacy, accountability, representation, and youth-led development initiatives. In addition, it provides an avenue for YORP members to receive assistance from the NYC and Local Youth Development Offices for their organizationsâ€™ activities.

The NYC also provided a walkthrough of the YORP online portal and a presentation on the registration process, requirements, compliance with the Seal of Good Local Governance (SGLG) youth governance indicators, and common concerns encountered in the procedures. An open forum allowed participants to ask their questions and seek clarification.

The NYC also continued to encourage participants to actively engage and strengthen collaboration not only with the government but also with other youth stakeholders. It was also underscored that when youth organizations are properly registered, recognized, and represented, they become stronger partners in governanceâ€”capable of contributing meaningfully to inclusive and youth-responsive local development.

#AreaNews | THE NATIONAL YOUTH COMMISSION CONDUCTS WEBINAR ON THE FILLING UP OF VACANCIES IN THE SANGGUNIANG KABATAAN

With the continued efforts to strengthen youth governance, the National Youth Commission (NYC), through the Naga Area Office, convened youth leaders for the â€œWebinar on the Conduct of Filling Up of Vacancies in the Sangguniang Kabataan (SK),â€ in alignment with the Department of the Interior and Local Government - National Youth Commission (DILG-NYC) Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1, Series of 2024. During the webinar, Local Youth Development Officers, SK officials, and local partners joined with a shared dedication to advance effective and responsive youth development mechanisms. 

The NYC Executive Director and COO, Leah T. Villalon, opened the meeting with an impactful message that underscored the importance of lawful and transparent procedures to maintain the integrity of youth governance. She noted that SK vacancies must be promptly and properly addressed to sustain effective local youth representation and leadership. 

The NYC - Naga Area Office presented the webinarâ€™s objectives and its rationale, emphasizing that following proper procedures in filling SK vacancies is necessary to prevent leadership gaps and ensure uninterrupted delivery of youth programs and services. 

Atty. Camille Britanico from the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) Region V provided a comprehensive discussion on the JMCâ€™s contents, such as the legal bases, step-by-step procedures, documentary requirements, and roles of concerned offices, and the common issues encountered by those undergoing the process. Finally, an open forum allowed participants to share their experiences and raise questions regarding the JMC.

In closing the program, the NYC - Naga Area Office encouraged SKs and youth development officers to remain compliant, collaborative, and proactive in upholding good governance and stressed the importance of leadership continuity as not only a procedural matter but a responsibility to those one serves.

LOOK | THE JUVENILE JUSTICE AND WELFARE COUNCIL (JJWC) SETS 2026 STRATEGIC DIRECTIONS TO FURTHER STRENGTHEN THE EXISTING JUVENILE JUSTICE FRAMEWORK

The Juvenile Justice and Welfare Council (JJWC) convened its first council meeting of the year on February 20, 2026, in Quezon City to establish a comprehensive roadmap for the months ahead. Presided over by Assistant Secretary Michelle Mae B. Gonzales of the National Youth Commission (NYC), the assembly prioritized defining key policy directions and identifying high-impact programs to fortify the countryâ€™s juvenile justice framework.

Among the key directions identified were the enhancement of restorative justice mechanisms, the strengthening of diversion programs at the local government level, and the continued monitoring and technical support for Bahay Pag-asa facilities nationwide. The Council also discussed the review and updating of existing guidelines to ensure alignment with current child protection standards and international best practices.

To further reinforce the juvenile justice framework, the JJWC committed to intensifying capacity-building initiatives for local social welfare officers, law enforcement personnel, and child protection stakeholders. The Council likewise emphasized improving inter-agency data sharing and case monitoring systems to ensure timely intervention, proper case handling, and improved rehabilitation outcomes for children at risk and children in conflict with the law.

Through these coordinated efforts, the JJWC aims to ensure that the juvenile justice system remains child-sensitive, rights-based, and focused on rehabilitation and reintegration.

LATEST | THE NATIONAL YOUTH COMMISSION AND THE OFFICE OF THE SOLICITOR GENERAL INK MEMORANDUM OF AGREEMENT TO STRENGTHEN GOVERNMENT TIES

The National Youth Commission (NYC) and the Office of the Solicitor General (OSG) have formalized their partnership through the signing of a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) last February 24, 2026, at the OSG Building in Makati City.

Leading the ceremony were the NYC Chairperson and CEO, Joseph Francisco â€œJeffâ€ Ortega, and the Solicitor General, Darlene Marie Berberabe, alongside other representatives from both agencies. 

The partnership opens avenues for collaborations in advancing legal literacy among the youth, including lectures on legal processes and potential partnership opportunities for aspiring legal professionals. Both agencies reaffirm their commitment to empowering young Filipinos with knowledge, access and meaningful opportunities in public service. 

The MOA serves as a testament to the importance of not only maintaining valuable partnerships but also coming together with a shared dedication to upholding institutional integrity in the government.

LOOK | THE NATIONAL YOUTH COMMISSION SHOWS SUPPORT FOR TEAM PINAS PARTNERâ€™S NIGHT

The National Youth Commission (NYC), alongside other government leaders, members of the diplomatic corps and academic institutions were some of the guests that were invited to the Partnerâ€™s Night launched by Team Pinas last February 19, 2026, at the Holiday Inn and Suites Makati.

Team Pinas is a multidisciplinary platform composed of Filipino scholars, policy experts, legal advocates, security specialists and civic organizers. It integrates policy research, public education initiatives, and cross-sector engagements to strengthen democratic institutions and promote diplomacy-centered approaches to national and global issues. Through policy discussions, learning forums and engagements with academic institutions and civic stakeholders, the organization seeks to translate research into sustained civic participation and informed public discourse.

The NYC recognizes the value of initiatives that promote policy literacy, democratic engagement, and constructive dialogue among young Filipinos. By supporting platforms that bridge research and grassroots participation, the Commission affirms its commitment to fostering critical thinking, responsible public engagement, and youth involvement in governance processes.

LATEST | THE NATIONAL YOUTH COMMISSION FORMALLY INDUCTS 2026â€“2028 SSEAYP INTERNATIONAL PHILIPPINES OFFICERS, AIMS TO STRENGTHEN GLOBAL YOUTH LEADERSHIP TIES

The National Youth Commission (NYC) Chairperson and Chief Executive Officer, Undersecretary Joseph Francisco â€œJeffâ€ Ortega, served as the inducting officer for the Ship for Southeast Asian and Japanese Youth Program (SSEAYP) International Philippines (SIP), Inc. induction and turnover ceremonies held last February 19, 2026, at the Bagong Pilipinas Headquarters in Mandaluyong City.

In his message, Chairperson Ortega challenged the incoming 2026â€“2028 officers and Board of Trustees to lead with integrity, innovation and inclusivity. He emphasized the vital role of globally engaged Filipino youth leaders in shaping responsive and forward-looking national development.

He highlighted that the SSEAYP has, over the years, produced generations of Filipino youth leaders who have gone on to serve in government, civil society, business, education and community development. EMPOWERING SK LEADERS: NATIONAL YOUTH COMMISSION STEPS UP YOUTH ACTION VS. ADOLESCENT PREGNANCY IN NORTHERN MINDANAO

The National Youth Commission (NYC) has reaffirmed its commitment to safeguarding and empowering young Filipinos through the approval of a resolution urging Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) officials in Northern Mindanao to actively promote and support the establishment of school-based teen centers. The resolution was approved ad referendum on December 23, 2025, and was sponsored by NYC Chairperson and CEO Undersecretary Joseph Francisco â€œJeffâ€ R. Ortega.

Anchored on Republic Act No. 8044, or the Youth in Nation-Building Act of 1995, the resolution reinforces the NYCâ€™s mandate to lead youth development policies and ensure meaningful youth participation in addressing issues that directly affect their welfare. Among the most urgent challenges confronting Region X (Northern Mindanao) is the persistently high rate of adolescent pregnancy.

Data from the Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) show that in Northern Mindanao, approximately 30 adolescents aged 15 to 19 give birth daily, while nearly six girls below 15 years old give birth each week. These figures represent the highest adolescent childbearing rate in the country, underscoring the need for immediate and coordinated action.

In response, President Ferdinand â€œBong Bongâ€ R. Marcos Jr. issued Presidential Directive PBBM No. 2025-1492 in April 2025, directing national and local government agencies to implement focused interventions addressing the root causes of adolescent pregnancy in the region. This mandate is reinforced by Executive Order (EO) No. 141, Series of 2021, which declared the prevention of teenage pregnancy a national priority and emphasized the vital roles of both the NYC and the SK in reversing this trend.

The resolution further builds on commitments made during the EO No. 141 Inter-Agency Technical Working Group Meeting held on November 21, 2025, where â€œProject 25@28: Reversing the Trend of Adolescent Pregnancy in Northern Mindanaoâ€ was presented. The initiative adopts a whole-of-government approach and identifies SK officials as key partners in mobilizing youth participation at the grassroots level.

By advocating for school-based teen centers, the NYC highlights the necessity of safe, accessible spaces where young people can receive accurate information and support. These centers are envisioned not only as educational venues but as platforms that empower youth to make informed choices and build healthier communities.

Aligned with the Philippine Youth Development Plan 2023â€“2028, the resolution underscores the importance of comprehensive sexuality education and responsive programming. Through Project 25@28, the NYC calls on Region 10â€™s SK officials to lead with purpose and compassionâ€”placing the youth at the heart of solutions for a safer, more hopeful future.

#AreaNews | NYC-CDO AREA OFFICE STRENGTHENS YOUTH LEADERSHIP THROUGH CAPACITY BUILDING WORKSHOP IN AGUSAN DEL NORTE

The National Youth Commission - Cagayan de Oro (NYC-CDO) Area Office successfully concluded a two-day Capacity Building Activity and Workshop for Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) officials and Local Youth Development Council (LYDC) members in Agusan del Norte, held on December 19-21, 2025, at Amontay Beach Resort.

The activity was organized by the Provincial Youth Development Office (PYDO) of Agusan del Norte, led by Mr. Alfer Fuentes, in coordination with Mr. Glynmer Jamito. It aimed to strengthen the legal and technical foundations of local youth leaders and enhance their capacity for effective youth governance.

Participants actively engaged in various sessions that highlighted the vital roles and functions of the SK, Local Youth Development Office (LYDO), and LYDC. The workshop also provided in-depth guidance on formulating the Comprehensive Barangay Youth Development Plan (CBYDP) and the Annual Barangay Youth Investment Program (ABYIP), ensuring alignment with national youth development frameworks.

A key highlight of the workshop was the hands-on technical session on the SK Web Portal. During this session, participants successfully registered their accounts and began encoding their Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) data for FY 2025 accomplishments. By utilizing digital reporting tools and properly documenting their accomplishments through appropriate Means of Verification (MOV), youth leaders are advancing transparency and accountability in their respective municipalities.

The NYC-CDO Area Office extends its sincere gratitude to Hon. Governor Ma. Angelica Rosedel Amante for her unwavering support and commitment to providing meaningful capacity-building opportunities for the youth leaders of Agusan del Norte.

The National Youth Commission (NYC) â€“ Pampanga Area Office successfully conducted the 4th Quarter Region III Central Luzon Sangguniang Kabataan - Regional Task Force (SK-RTF) and the Philippine Youth Development Plan - Regional Inter-Agency Committee (PYDP-RIAC) meetings on December 2â€“3, 2025, at Hungry Surfer Alley, Baler, Aurora. The PYDP-RIAC Meeting, held the following day, opened with the same preliminaries and a short rapport-building activity. After confirming a quorum, Mr. Aguinaldo delivered the opening message, underscoring the shared duty of agencies and youth-serving institutions in implementing the Philippine Youth Development Plan. Youth sector partners also shared their accomplishments, such as review sessions, anti-drug initiatives, Lakbay Ugnayan activities, climate action campaigns, and youth recognition programs. The next PYDP-RIAC Meeting was announced for February 2026 in Lucena City.

The collaboration between the NYC - Baguio Area Office and the City Youth Development Office successfully emphasized the importance of transparency, accountability, and leadership in local youth governance. The Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) Region IV-A, in coordination with the Department of Environment and Natural Resources (DENR) Region IV-A, the National Youth Commission (NYC) - CALABARZON, and the University of the Philippines â€“ Los BaÃ±os (UPLB) Ugnayan ng Pahinungod, successfully conducted the â€œEmpowering Youth Stewards: Manila Bay Clean-Up, Rehabilitation, and Preservation Program (MBCRPP) and Operation LISTO Orientation for SK Officials in CALABARZONâ€ on October 27â€“29, 2025, at Forest Crest, Nasugbu, Batangas.

â€ŽThe 13th NYP, originally set for November 12â€“16, 2025, will now take place on November 17â€“21, 2025. This adjustment was made after careful consideration of the latest weather advisories and the safety of everyone involved.

On October 11, more than 120 youth leaders, advocates, and government officials gathered for the National Local Conference of Youth (LCOY) Philippines 2025, united by one goal: putting children and youth at the forefront of Climate Action and Disaster Resilience (CADR).

For the first time, LCOY reached across the archipelago, from Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao, to hold Regional LCOYs, amplifying local voices and co-creating Regional Youth Statements that reflect diverse realities and aspirations. These regional statements were then consolidated into one National Youth Statement for CADR, representing the unified call of the Filipino youth.

The National LCOY featured the presentation of the National Youth Statement, a panel discussion with representatives from DepEd, the Climate Change Commission, DENR, and the Council for the Welfare of Children, and engaging breakout sessions such as:

Lessons on Youth Disaster Response and Urban Resilience
Creative Resistance in the Climate Advocacy
Stories on the Ground: Redefining True Resilience
Everyday Climate Choices: Navigating Personal Responsibility and Accountability
Lobbying for Reforms: Creating Space for Youth in Climate Policymaking

#AreaNews | NYC - BAGUIO AREA OFFICE JOINS TRAINING ON CHR MANDATE AND HUMAN RIGHTS TO STRENGTHEN YOUTH EMPOWERMENT

Government information officers, media practitioners, and agency representatives from the Cordillera Administrative Region gathered at the Orchard Hotel last September 30, 2025 for a training on the Commission on Human Rightsâ€™ (CHR) mandate, programs, services, and the fundamental principles of human rights. The National Youth Commission (NYC) - Baguio Area Office, represented by Dr. Sheridan Athena Gajete, participated in discussions that highlighted the importance of upholding and promoting human rights as a cornerstone of democracy and inclusive development.

The training, led by CHR-Cordillera Administrative Region officials, opened with a welcome message from Atty. Lyndon Morales, Head of the CHRâ€™s Legal Division. Ms. Charmaine Joy Batong-Ambros walked participants through the mandates, programs, projects, and activities of the CHR, while Ms. Rizel Villanueva discussed the basics of human rights. Central to the training was clarifying misconceptions about the CHR, stressing that it is not an anti-government institution but an independent constitutional office mandated to monitor the governmentâ€™s compliance with human rights treaties and obligations.

Participants were reminded that human rights violations are often committed when state actors abuse authority, disproportionately affecting vulnerable groups such as Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, senior citizens, and marginalized communities. In contrast, crimes committed by civilians against each other are addressed under criminal law, not by the CHR. This distinction highlighted their crucial role in ensuring government accountability.

The CHR emphasized that protecting human rights is a shared responsibility, with the state, as duty-bearer, must respect and uphold the rights of all, while citizens, as rights-holders, must also respect one anotherâ€™s rights. More than legal concepts, human rights were framed as the foundation of social progress, democracy, and nation-building. The training further urged responsible and informed reporting from journalists and government communicators, stressing that accurate and human rights-based narratives help build trust, transparency, and a culture of dignity.

PRESS RELEASE | Youth voices are shaping the updating of the Philippinesâ€™ NDC

Metro Manila, Philippines - Young peopleâ€™s voices are at the forefront of the Philippinesâ€™ efforts to ensure the inclusive and ambitious updating of its Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC), the countryâ€™s official climate pledge under the Paris Agreement. 

The NDC outlines the countryâ€™s plans and targets to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and strengthen resilience to the impacts of climate change. The country first submitted its NDC in 2021 where it committed to a 75% reduction in emissions by 2030, primarily conditional on international support and covering key sectors including energy, transport, agriculture, waste, and industry. The NDC is required to be updated every five years.

The Climate Change Commission (CCC), Department of Environment and Natural Resources (DENR), and National Youth Commission (NYC), with the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) Philippines, supported a series of youth consultations spanning six days across Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao. These engagements brought together youth-led and youth-serving organizations tackling climate change and NDC-related issues, as well as representatives from the Sangguniang Kabataan (SK), Local Youth Development Offices (LYDOs), and Boy Scouts of the Philippines (BSP).

A total of 119 participants joined the interactive workshops, policy simulations, and consultative dialogues conducted in the cities of Manila, Iloilo, and Davao from 18-22 August 2025. The process resulted in the drafting of a Youth Pledge to Climate Action which unified recommendations and language intended to directly inform the drafting of inclusive and representative policies and measures (PAMs) and the forthcoming updated NDC text.

Commissioner Rachel Anne Herrera of the CCC emphasized that the consultations carried out as part of the steps in updating the NDC were essential components of the Philippinesâ€™ commitment to address climate change. 

â€œClimate change is not a distant concern; it is a present-day challenge that disproportionately affects young Filipinos. As the generation that will inherit the impacts of our decisions today, your voices as youth leaders are indispensable in shaping effective and equitable climate policies,â€ she said.

Chairperson Joseph Francisco â€œJeffâ€ Ortega of the NYC expressed support for the youth leaders who participated in the week-long consultation process and urged fellow government leaders to treat the youth as co-creators of climate solutions. â€œSa ating mga kabataan [To our youth], I commend you for crafting a unified Youth Pledge to Climate Action because this pledge is not just a symbol; it is a declaration of leadership, responsibility, and hope. Meaning to say, we are owning the problem and weâ€™re trying to do something about it. Let us use this big responsibility, this voice, to make that change,â€ he said.

Meanwhile, Director Elenida DR. Basug of the Climate Change Service of DENR stressed how climate action is a shared responsibility where the youthâ€™s insights would be invaluable. â€œThe NDC represents our nationâ€™s commitment to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and adapt to the impacts of climate change. It is our roadmap for a sustainable future, but they will only be effective if they reflect the needs, priorities, and innovative ideas of all Filipinos, especially the youth,â€ she said.

Similarly, Dr. Selva Ramachandran, UNDP Philippines Resident Representative, underscored that co-creating the NDC updates with the help of young people and gender equality, disability, and social inclusion (GEDSI) stakeholders and advocates guarantees that the country comes up with â€œtruly inclusive climate commitments that not only ensure that no one is left behind but, most importantly, prioritize the voices of those who are furthest behind.â€

By placing young voices at the heart of the NDC updating process, the Philippines is demonstrating that climate ambition and climate justice go hand in hand in the efforts to ensure that the countryâ€™s climate commitments reflect both urgency and inclusivity for present and future generations.

#AreaNews | NYC - BAGUIO JOINS THIRD QUARTER INTER-AGENCY MEETING TO ADVANCE CHILD PROTECTION AND JUSTICE IN CORDILLERA

The National Youth Commission (NYC) - Baguio Area Office actively took part in the Third Quarter Joint Inter-Agency Committee Meeting of the Regional Juvenile Justice and Welfare Committee (RJJWC-CAR), Regional Sub-Committee for the Welfare of Children (RSCWC-CAR), and the Regional Committee Against Trafficking and Violence Against Women and their Children (RCAT-VAWC-CAR) held on September 17, 2025, at H100 Ecolodge, Baguio City.

Presided over by Prosecutor Carlo Carantes of the Department of Justice â€“ Office of the Regional Prosecutor I, the meeting highlighted the shared commitment of the government and partner agencies to reinforce the implementation of Republic Act No. 9344, as amended, also known as the Juvenile Justice and Welfare Act (JJWA). The convergence sought to strengthen collective efforts in building a more protective, responsive, and child-sensitive justice system for the children of the Cordillera region.

Highlights of the meeting included the presentation of draft guidelines on awards and recognition for Local Government Units (LGUs) and stakeholders who excel in implementing the JJWA. This initiative aims to institutionalize the acknowledgment of outstanding practices, inspire wider compliance, and encourage innovation in child protection programs. The committee also endorsed a joint resolution urging LGUs to hire or designate licensed social workers solely assigned to handle Children in Need of Special Protection (CNSP) cases to address the pressing gaps in local service delivery and personnel support.

The committee likewise reviewed consolidated issues and challenges identified during recent monitoring and technical assistance activities with LGUs, shedding light on capacity, funding, and coordination constraints that hinder the full realization of child welfare programs. Initial preparations for the observance of Juvenile Justice and Welfare Consciousness Week in October and National Childrenâ€™s Month in November were also set in motion, as they featured activities centered on child rights education, advocacy campaigns, and raising public awareness.

This inter-agency effort emphasized the value of collaboration in advancing holistic, rights-based solutions to safeguard children, especially those in conflict with the law or facing vulnerable situations. It reflects a united call to action: to protect, empower, and give every child the fair chance they deserve.

READ | DILG to Review Policies on LGU Travel

Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) Secretary Jonvic Remulla announced on Tuesday that the DILG will review the policies governing government-funded travel for local government officials, including those for the Sangguniang Kabataan (SK).

In a media interview, Remulla suggested that it may be time to reassess the current policy, emphasizing that all government-funded travel should serve a legitimate purpose. "Maybe it's time to review the policy to ensure that all travel, if government-funded, is legitimate and serves a specific purpose," he said.

His remarks followed controversy surrounding posts by Sangguniang Kabataan members from the City of Manila, who shared about their supposed "capacity development" and "international benchmarking" activities in Thailand, funded by SK funds. The posts sparked backlash from netizens, who criticized the trip as a mere junket.

In response, Remulla revealed that the DILG plans to issue a memorandum circular to clarify rules surrounding the use of public funds for travel. "We need to carefully examine the language, particularly when it comes to where the funds should or should not come from," he said. "We also need to specify the clearances required for such trips."

Reflecting on his own experience as governor of Cavite, Remulla shared that he never allowed such overseas training programs. "Training and capacity-building abroad, I don't think thatâ€™s necessary," he explained.
"What surprised me most was the way they openly flaunted their good time, posting about it repeatedly," Remulla continued. "It's part of a larger culture of trying to impress others, and thatâ€™s ultimately what led to all of this."

The National Youth Commission (NYC)-Baguio Area Office, in partnership with the City Youth Development Office (CYDO) of Baguio, has officially launched the 2nd Semester Monitoring for Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) in the city. The initiative began on September 5, 2025, with the first visit being held in Barangay Fort del Pilar, one of the pilot areas selected for this round of assessments.

Representing the NYC-Baguio Area Office was Mr. Michael Bernal, joined by the CYDOâ€™s Ms. Vanessa Landa Olson and Ms. Francis Ann Cheska Delim. Together, the team carried out the monitoring to strengthen youth governance and ensure SKs are fully aligned with national policies on programs, documentation, and implementation.

The visit focused on reviewing key requirements, such as the Comprehensive Barangay Youth Development Plan (CBYDP), Annual Barangay Youth Investment Program (ABYIP), the SK Annual Budget, the Sangguniang Kabataan Financial and Database Book (SKFDB), and KK (Katipunan ng Kabataan) Profiling. These serve as vital tools to guarantee transparency, accountability, and the effective rollout of youth-driven initiatives at the grassroots level.

Beyond compliance, the activity also provided an opportunity for capacity building. Mr. Bernal facilitated a brief orientation on Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) Department of Budget Management - Department of the Interior and Local Government - National Youth Commission (DBM-DILG-NYC) No. 1, Series of 2025, which introduced updated guidelines and compliance measures. The session emphasized the importance of strategic planning, responsible budgeting, and proper documentation to help SK officials lead effectively and responsibly.

The monitoring, which runs from August until October 2025, goes beyond simply checking requirements. It embodies the shared commitment of NYC and CYDO to empower young leaders, nurture their skills, and uphold meaningful youth participation in governance. With every visit, the initiative becomes more than just monitoringâ€”it becomes a platform to inspire responsible leadership, accountability, and service among todayâ€™s youth leaders.

In recent years, HIV/AIDS has increasingly affected Filipino youth, making it one of the most urgent health and social challenges of our time.

The National Youth Commission (NYC) conducted the Ship for Southeast Asia Youth Program (SSEAYP) Alumni Night of the Pre-departure Training (PDT) of the 49th SSEAYP, held on September 6, 2025, at Tagaytay City, Cavite. Attending the event were SSEAYP Alumni from different batches, SSEAYP officials from the Philippines and Singapore, and the sixteen (16) 49th SSEAYP Participating Youth (PYs) from Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao.

Dr. Herbert Glenn P. Reyes, 49th SSEAYP PDT Lead Facilitator, delivered the opening remarks as he shared his experience during the PDT and the crucial skills and values needed by the Philippine delegates in their journey, namely active learning and listening, communication, leadership, goal-setting, problem-solving, trust, etiquette, and unity. Dr. Reyes also presented the training team and former SSEAYP Alumni and PDT Facilitators Engr. Dion Mendoza, Dr. Jasper Ruby Vijar, and Ms. Zyjazz De Guzman.

The keynote speaker, NYC Commissioner representing Luzon, Assistant Secretary Gervy James Gumarit, talked about how â€œSSEAYP has been a bridge for how many years, where the youth can dream, act, and grow together.â€ He stressed the importance of authentic representation and championing the Philippine Youth Development Plan and affirmed the commitment of the NYC to stand with young leaders, especially the SSEAYP delegates, and help the youth amplify their voices. Lastly, he extended his well-wishes to everyone in their endeavors.

To share a message of support, the SSEAYP International Philippines, Inc. President, Ms. Bevy Cuenca-Caral (former 1995 PY) thanked NYC for hosting the alumni night and motivated the young leaders with words of wisdom to stay active, as she said that â€œThe future belongs to the consistent, not the lacking, not the talented, but the one who shows up.â€ She reiterated how important it is to show gratitude, respect, and humility generously.

The 49th SSEAYP delegation showcased a National Presentation, which featured a performance on a story about the Philippines and a â€œSisiwitâ€, a traditional dance from the Cordillera region. Afterward, the 48th SSEAYP (Batch Hiraya) Youth Leader, Mr. Jefferson Hilario, officially turned over the representation of the Philippines to the new PYs and urged them to â€œEmbrace the voyage with the same passion, humility, and resilience.â€ Finally, he advised them to â€œLet the Hiraya spirit guide youâ€ and to make the nation proud through their representation.

The closing remarks were given by the 49th SSEAYP National Leader, Mr. Metodio Maraguinot, Jr., who highlighted that â€œWe may be different, but we share one heart,â€ and that every person has to trust and believe in one another. With the closing of the SSEAYP PDT, the delegation is set to engage in the next step of their SSEAYP journey, the Pre-Departure Activities, such as courtesy visits to various institutions and key officials.

The Association of La Union Youth Development Officers (ALUYDO) convened its 3rd Quarter Meeting in the Municipality of Naguilian, La Union, bringing together Youth Development Officers (YDOs) from the various cities and municipalities of the province. The gathering aimed to enhance coordination, discuss policy updates, and strategize on the continuous advancement of youth development programs in La Union.

Representing the National Youth Commission (NYC)-Baguio Area Office, Dr. Sheridan Athena Gajete shared the latest Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) policy updates, emphasizing the importance of ensuring that youth councils remain responsive and aligned with national priorities. She also presented the official guidelines for the training of Local Youth Development Councils (LYDCs), which will serve as a framework for capacitating youth organizations to be more effective partners of local government units in governance and development initiatives. â€œStrengthening LYDCs is a crucial step in amplifying the voices of our young people. With proper training and support, they can become vital partners in shaping policies and programs that directly impact their communities,â€ Dr. Gajete said during her presentation.

Dr. Jerome Acupan, President of ALUYDO, formally opened the meeting by delivering his welcome remarks, where he underscored the collective responsibility of YDOs in nurturing the next generation of leaders. He also laid down the next steps of the association, which included continuous professional development for YDOs, intensified collaboration with local government units, and stronger partnerships with youth organizations. â€œAs development officers, we carry the responsibility of guiding and supporting our young leaders. By working together as an association, we can harmonize our efforts and ensure that the youth of La Union are given the opportunities, skills, and platforms they need to thrive,â€ Dr. Acupan remarked. 

The meeting also provided a venue for members to share best practices, discuss challenges in their localities, and identify strategies to strengthen youth participation in governance further. #49thSSEAYP | The Journey to SSEAYP: Philippine Youth Leaders Undergo Pre-Departure Training in Preparation for Japan-ASEAN Voyage
  
Today marks the fifth day of the Ship for Southeast Asian Youth and Japanese Program (SSEAYP) finalists as they continue their journey to become the official representatives of the Philippines to 49th SSEAYP. 

After hurdling the stringent selection process, sixteen youth leaders and one national leader are currently undergoing the Pre-Departure Training (PDT) aimed at establishing bond among the finalists and build team cohesion.

Once confirmed, the finalists, coming from varying backgrounds, will join other youth delegates from Southeast Asia and Japan in a month-long program aimed at enhancing cooperation and understanding among the young people of ASEAN and Japan.

The finalists are guided by this yearâ€™s facilitators from previous SSEAYP batches. Dr. Herbert Glenn Reyes, SSEAYP Batch 1993, leads the training team in ensuring that this yearâ€™s batch continue the long-standing tradition of outstanding performance by the Philippine contingent. Dr. Reyes is joined by Dr. Jasper Ruby Vijar (SSEAYP Batch 2017), Engineer Dion Michael Mendoza (SSEAYP Batch 2019), and Ms. Zyjazz de Guzman (SSEAYP Batch 2024).

The National Youth Commission (NYC) leads the selection, training, and deployment of the Philippine contingent to SSEAYP. This is an annual cultural exchange program sponsored by the Government of Japan and supported by ASEAN member states. It annually brings together 177 youth delegates from Japan and Southeast Asia in a month-long cultural and educational journey across Japan and Southeast Asia.

#AreaNews | NYC BAGUIO PARTICIPATES IN FIRST REGIONAL POPULATION EXECUTIVE BOARD MEETING IN CORDILLERA

The National Youth Commission (NYC)- Baguio Area Office participated in the first Regional Population Executive Board (RPEB) meeting of the Cordillera Administrative Region (CAR) for 2025. The gathering, spearheaded by Director Apollo Pagano of the Commission on Population and Development (CPD) and co-chaired by Regional Director Cecille Basawil of CPD-CAR, united key government agencies and stakeholders to tackle critical population and development challenges within the region.

NYC Baguio, represented by Dr. Sheridan Athena Y. Gajete, championed the inclusion of youth perspectives in regional policy development, particularly concerning the region's demographic future. Their active involvement in the RPEB ensures that the needs and aspirations of young Filipinos remain a top priority in the creation and execution of population programs. NYC Baguio voiced strong support for policies that foster collaboration across generations, inclusive planning, and responsive governance, which considers the dynamic needs of the youth in the Cordillera.

The RPEB meeting addressed a comprehensive agenda, demonstrating a commitment to strategic planning and evidence-based decision-making. High on the list was the meticulous review and approval of the RPEB Work and Financial Plan (WFP), which will set the stage for focused action and resource allocation throughout the year. Recognizing the importance of well-being at all ages, the board also engaged in discussions surrounding the Regional Plan for Healthy and Active Ageing, aimed to support the elderly within the context of evolving demographics.

In a proactive move to address shifting population trends, a proposed monitoring tool for negative population growth was introduced, empowering local governments to better understand and respond to this crucial issue. A landmark agenda item was the endorsement of proposed resolutions urging local government units (LGUs) to establish Local Population Committees, integrate Population Officers into their Local Planning Boards, and embed population and development considerations at the heart of local policy-making.

The body also undertook a thorough review of the latest insights from the 2024 Census and the Civil Registration and Vital Statistics (CRVS), underscoring the importance of data-driven strategies. Updates on the upcoming celebration of World Population Day and the recognition of the Rafael Salas Regional Winner were shared, which highlighted regional achievements and fostered public awareness. The meeting further delved into the activities of the National Subcommittee on Migration and Development and the increasing complexities of internal and external migration. Adding a fresh dimension to their efforts, the CPD introduced its new branding, signaling a renewed commitment to effective public communication and engagement with stakeholders. The active participation of NYC Baguio in this significant regional forum underscored the vital role of youth in shaping the future of the Cordillera.

#AreaNews | NYC ADVOCATES FOR CHILDREN AND YOUTHâ€™S RIGHTS IN CLEAR CAR PROJECT IN BAGUIO CITY 

The  National Youth Commission (NYC) actively participated in the Child Labor â€“ Effective Awareness and Response in the Cordillera Administrative Region (CLEAR CAR) Project Stakeholder Dialogue (Baseline Report Presentation and Planning Workshop) last August 5, 2025, at Sotto Grande Hotel, Baguio City. The event served as a platform for the projectâ€™s baseline report presentation and planning workshop, aimed at mobilizing stakeholders for collaborative action against child labor in the region. The activity sought to raise awareness on the persistent issue of child labor, deepen stakeholdersâ€™ understanding of relevant child protection laws and mechanisms, and reinforce community-based responses. 

Approximately 75 participants from a diverse range of sectors involved in child protection and youth development attended the function, including barangay officials, representatives from the Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE), the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office (MSWDO), regional line agencies, youth leaders, civil society organizations, and community advocates. 

The program began with opening remarks from Ms. Cherryl Balaoadan, CLEAR CAR Coordinator, who emphasized the value of multisectoral collaboration in addressing child labor. This was followed by presentations from DOLE and child rights advocates, focusing on the current child labor situation in the Cordillera region, observation of relevant factors contributing to child labor, national policies and legal frameworks, and best practices for prevention and intervention. 

During the planning workshop, participants identified child labor risks specific to their localities and proposed community-based strategies to address them. A draft community action plan was developed as an initial output of the workshop. Despite the productive outcomes, several challenges were identified, including the limited reach of information campaigns in remote areas and the need to strengthen referral systems at the barangay level. To address these concerns, the participants recommended conducting localized orientations, creating barangay-level monitoring teams, and institutionalizing continuous inter-agency collaboration under the CLEAR CAR framework.

An open forum encouraged meaningful dialogue and sharing of grassroots experiences, and the event culminated in a collective pledge to support the goals of the CLEAR CAR Project. This initiative successfully fostered a stronger sense of responsibility among participants, who expressed a commitment to take a more proactive role in identifying, reporting, and responding to child labor cases. As a youth development agency, NYC underscored the importance of youth participation in community efforts to combat child labor and pledged to continue supporting awareness-raising and capacity-building initiatives with and for the youth.

In his opening message, Iriga City Mayor Hon. Wilfredo Rex C. #AreaNews | NYC NAGA OFFICE DELIVERS LOCAL MONITORING AND EVALUATION COACHING SESSION TO STRENGTHEN YOUTH GOVERNANCE IN REGION V 

The National Youth Commission (NYC) Naga Area Office spearheaded the Local Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) Coaching Session last July 16, 2025, at the Bicol Expo Hall, Legazpi City Convention Center. The program served as a significant capacity-building initiative, equipping young leaders with the knowledge and tools to strengthen their roles in youth governance and development planning, particularly in preparation for the 2025 Local Youth Development Plan (LYDP). 51 youth development stakeholders across the Bicol Region attended the session, which comprised the regionâ€™s Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) officials, Local Youth Development Officers (LYDOs), and members of the Local Youth Development Council (LYDC) from various Local Government Units (LGUs). 

Benito Lot Jr. O. Reyes, NYC Presidential Staff Officer, delivered an informative LGU status update on the current state of LYDO appointments, LYDP adoption, and LYDC activity levels in Region V. His presentation highlighted both accomplishments and challenges, where LGUs were seen to have a lack of formally appointed LYDOs or fully-functioning LYDCs. He emphasized the importance of compliance with Republic Act No. 10742 and other relevant issuances to ensure more inclusive and participatory youth involvement. Furthermore, Mr. Reyes discussed the SK Web Portal (SKWP), designed to streamline submission and tracking of youth development documents, which was seen to be underutilized in several LGUs, as only 53 SK Federation Presidents had active accounts. As such, it was emphasized that there is a need for intensified digital onboarding and training at the local level to bridge existing gaps.

Ms. Nydia P. Delfin, Cluster Head for Region V, facilitated the discussion on the LGU Readiness Checklist for LYDP Preparation, which evaluates LGU preparedness across key indicators, such as institutional setup, youth participation, resource allocation, data readiness, and policy support. Her presentation underscored the importance of evidence-based planning and the localization of youth development strategies. She also introduced the SK Functionality Assessment Tool, a comprehensive checklist to monitor and evaluate the performance of SKs, LYDOs, and LYDCs, based on documentation compliance and functional outputs, to encourage consistent adherence to minimum standards and accountability mechanisms.

Mr. Louis Rhey V. Adan, Presidential Staff Officer, discussed the reporting and compliance requirements for LGUs by providing clear guidelines on the submission of LYDC quarterly meeting minutes, proper process for LYDP endorsement and approval, and necessary documentary evidence for compliance. Also presented was the official SK timeline for 2025, detailing all mandatory activities and submissions aligned with NYC and DILG directives, serving as a roadmap for SK officials and LYDOs to manage their responsibilities effectively and promptly. 

Representatives from the Municipalities of Camalig and Manito, Albay also shared best practices that have institutionalized financial support for their LYDC members participating in national and international youth eventsâ€“such as allocation of travel allowances and pocket moneyâ€“demonstrating a strong commitment to empowering youth leaders beyond local boundaries and encouraging other LGUs to consider similar strategies to support youth representation and leadership development.

This serves as a testament to NYCâ€™s commitment to nurturing a generation of young leaders who are not only policy-aware but also proactive in shaping the future of their communities. The session capacitated youth development stakeholders by promoting digital transformation in local youth governance and reinforcing accountability and transparency in program implementation. The NYC calls on all members to maintain the momentum of collaboration, uphold the principles of good governance, and ensure that youth development remains a priority in local planning and implementation.

#AreaNews | Davao de Oro SK Leaders Strengthen Fiscal Management Skills in Provincial Summit

Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) leaders from across Davao de Oro gathered for the SK Summit 2025, a province-wide initiative designed to enhance their financial management skills and promote fiscal independence in youth governance. The summit provided a critical platform for learning and collaboration among young public servants.

The event featured a series of intensive capacity-building sessions facilitated by experts from the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) and other key agencies. Discussions focused on essential topics including budget preparation, procurement processes, and financial reporting in compliance with Commission on Audit (COA) procedures. The training clarified the distinct financial roles and responsibilities of SK officials, Local Youth Development Officers (LYDOs), and Local Chief Executives (LCEs), reinforcing accountability and transparency in handling public funds.

Mr. Russell Bonn Noval of the National Youth Commission (NYC) Davao Area Office graced the summit, sharing updates on national SK policies and youth governance reforms. His session, held in collaboration with representatives from the Technical Education and Skills Development Authority (TESDA), addressed pressing questions from SK officials on policy implementation, skills training opportunities, and broader youth empowerment programs, linking local efforts to the national agenda.

The successful convergence was a result of strong collaborative leadership. LGOO VII Ma. Aurora C. Corpuz, OICâ€“Provincial Director of DILG Davao de Oro, was lauded for her excellent and dedicated guidance over the event. The seamless execution was also made possible by the vital support of the Provincial Youth Development Office, under the direction of Sir Alexis Micutuan. The initiative was championed by Hon. Noriel John P. Ramacula, SK Provincial Federation President, whose commitment to youth development was instrumental in bringing the summit to fruition.

The active engagement of SK Municipal Federation Presidents from all 11 municipalities and SK Chairpersons from various barangays underscored a unified commitment to elevating the standards of youth leadership and public service throughout the province.

#AreaNews | DILG Region 8 and NYC Strengthen Youth Governance Through "SKwela" Capacity Building Program

The Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) Region 8, in collaboration with the National Youth Commission (NYC) Region 7 and 8 Cluster, successfully concluded "SKwela: Equipping SKs and LYDOs for LYDP, CBYDP, and ABYIP Formulation." Held via Zoom, this vital online capacity-building session brought together Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) officials, Local Youth Development Officers (LYDOs), and DILG Focal and Field Officers to enhance their technical knowledge and planning skills.

The initiative aimed to ensure the quality formulation of crucial youth development plans, including the Local Youth Development Plan (LYDP), Comprehensive Barangay Youth Development Plan (CBYDP), and Annual Barangay Youth Investment Program (ABYIP). This aligns with the implementation of the SK Full Public Disclosure (SKFPD) Policy and the broader national goals outlined in the Philippine Youth Development Plan (PYDP).

Mr. Adrian Thomas E. Colasito, Assistant Chief of DILG-LGMED, formally opened the session, emphasizing the importance of proactive and participatory planning in youth governance. Ms. Cherry Mae Malate, PSO IV and Cluster Head of NYC Region 7 and 8, followed with a message of support, reaffirming NYCâ€™s commitment to empowering SK officials and LYDOs for evidence-based, youth-centered planning.

The learning discussions provided comprehensive insights into youth development planning. Ms. Cherry Mae Malate elucidated the framework and priorities of the PYDP and LYDP, highlighting the need for local plans to align with national targets while addressing unique local needs. Ms. Cybel C. Tantay, PSO I of NYC, then detailed the technical processes, templates, and steps for formulating CBYDPs and ABYIPs, underscoring the significance of data collection, stakeholder consultations, and effective project identification. Mr. Mitch S. Vargas, PSO I â€“ DMME, concluded the sessions by guiding participants through the SK Web Portal, presenting Region 8's submission status, and stressing the importance of timely compliance, data accuracy, and proactive encoding by SK secretaries and LYDOs.

The engaging Open Forum segment addressed critical and recurring concerns raised by participants. Technical difficulties in accessing and navigating the SK Web Portal, particularly regarding data encoding and document uploads, were highlighted, prompting requests for refresher orientations or user guides. Another pressing concern revolved around the proper utilization of SK funds, specifically aligning projects with approved CBYDP and ABYIP, and seeking clarification on budget programming and expenditure tracking in compliance with audit and policy guidelines. Challenges in filling vacant SK official positions due to resignations, migration, or disinterest were also shared by several LGUs. In response, NYC and DILG committed to issuing relevant advisories and providing follow-up technical assistance to address these bottlenecks.

The "SKwela" session served as a valuable platform for capacity building, mutual learning, policy clarification, and coordination. It also reinforced the critical partnership between DILG, NYC, and local youth stakeholders in advancing youth participation and ensuring the effective delivery of youth development initiatives.

#AreaNews | â€œSIGE NA!â€ Webinar Series Sparks Youth-Led Action for Gender Equality in Bicol

In celebration of Pride Month, the two-part â€œSIGE NA! (Sulong Inisyatiba para sa Gender Equality sa Bicolandia!)â€ Webinar Series brought together 168 youth stakeholders, including Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) officials, Local Youth Development Officers (LYDOs), Local Youth Development Council (LYDC) members, and youth organization representatives from across the Bicol Region, to deepen their understanding of gender equality and inclusivity.

Held on June 17, 2025, the first session featured Mx. Kahel Rebadola, who delivered a compelling discussion on the core concepts of SOGIESCâ€”Sexual Orientation, Gender Identity and Expression, and Sex Characteristics. Framed within a human rights lens, the session highlighted the principles of equality, non-discrimination, and dignity for all. Participants were encouraged to critically examine personal biases, understand intersectionality with other identities such as race, class, religion, and disability, and embrace inclusive language. The discussion also addressed how stigma and discrimination directly impact the mental health and well-being of LGBTQIA+ individuals.

The second session, held on June 24, 2025, featured renowned LGBTQIA+ rights advocate Mx. Ging Cristobal, who delivered an experience-driven session on LGBTQIA+ Rights and Health. Drawing from their advocacy work in Quezon City, they shed light on systemic barriers and everyday discrimination faced by the LGBTQIA+ community, particularly in healthcare, legal protection, and access to social services. Mx. Cristobal also shared best practices and replicable initiatives promoting inclusive governance and rights-based service delivery. Their session inspired participants to take meaningful action in their own localities.

More than a learning platform, the SIGE NA! Webinar Series served as a catalyst for inclusive youth leadership in the region. A joint initiative by the NYC Naga Area Office, the Provincial Government of Sorsogon, the Sorsogon Youth Development Office, Sorsogon Gender and Development Office, Sorsogon Social Welfare and Development Office, Youth in Action Sorsogon, and Free to Be Youth Philippines, the series empowered young leaders to integrate gender-sensitive and rights-based perspectives into local governance and youth programming.

The National Youth Commission (NYC) has issued an advisory, calling on all members of the Youth Organization Registration Program (YORP) and National Youth Volunteers Program (NYVP) aged 18 to 30 years old to register for the upcoming Barangay and Sangguniang Kabataan Elections (BSKE).

Voter registration for the BSKE will officially commence in the last week of October 2025 and continue until July 2026. The NYC emphasizes the critical role of youth in shaping the future of their communities through active participation in the democratic process.

#PrideMonth2025 | NYC AFFIRMS COMMITMENT TO LGBTQIA+ YOUTH RIGHTS, FOSTERS A MORE INCLUSIVE FUTURE FOR ALL FILIPINO YOUTH

In a significant move towards fostering a truly inclusive environment for all young Filipinos, and in celebration of Pride Month, the National Youth Commission (NYC) proudly announces the passage of a landmark resolution supporting the rights and well-being of Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer, Intersex, Asexual, and other Sexually Oriented and Gender Diverse (LGBTQIA+) youth in the Philippines. The resolution, sponsored by Chairperson and CEO Joseph Francisco Ortega, emphasizes NYCâ€™s dedication to creating a society where every young person can flourish, regardless of their sexual orientation, gender identity, or expression (SOGIE).

As the sole policy-making and coordinating body for all youth-related government initiatives, the NYC recognizes its pivotal role in ensuring the holistic development and protection of the nation's youth. This resolution reaffirms the principles enshrined in the 1987 Philippine Constitution and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, both of which uphold the inherent rights of all individuals without discrimination.

"This Pride Month, we are not just celebrating diversity; we are actively legislating for a more equitable future," stated Chairperson Ortega. "Our resolution is a declaration that the National Youth Commission stands firmly with our LGBTQIA+ youth. We acknowledge the persistent challenges they face â€“ discrimination, exclusion, bullying, and violence â€“ and we are committed to dismantling these barriers. Our goal is to ensure that every young Filipino, irrespective of their SOGIE, is empowered to reach their full potential and contribute meaningfully to national development."

Despite existing legal and policy commitments, many LGBTQIA+ young people in the Philippines continue to grapple with significant hurdles. These experiences profoundly impact their mental health, disrupt their education, limit their career prospects, and diminish their overall well-being. A 2021 study by the Philippine Social Science Council on the experiences of LGBTQI+ Filipinos found that 73% of LGBTQI+ individuals reported experiencing some form of discrimination, with youth being particularly vulnerable in educational and familial settings. Furthermore, the lack of comprehensive national legislation and inclusive policies has historically marginalized LGBTQIA+ youth, creating systemic barriers to their full participation in society.

The NYC believes that embracing and celebrating the diversity of its people, especially its youth, in all their identities and expressions, is fundamental to a thriving and compassionate society. This resolution is a tangible step towards achieving that vision.

Key commitments outlined in the resolution include championing, uplifting, and safeguarding the rights and well-being of LGBTQIA+ youth across the Philippines and actively mainstreaming SOGIE-inclusive principles into the formulation and implementation of all youth development policies, programs, and projects.

"To build a truly inclusive youth, we must actively address the unique needs and challenges faced by our LGBTQIA+ young people," added Chairperson Ortega. "This resolution is a call to action. It signifies our dedication to creating a safe, supportive, and empowering environment where every young Filipino feels valued, respected, and free to be their authentic self. We invite all stakeholders â€“ government agencies, civil society organizations, families, and communities â€“ to join us in this vital endeavor."

The National Youth Commission looks forward to working collaboratively with various sectors to implement the provisions of this resolution, ensuring a future where all young Filipinos, including those from the LGBTQIA+ community, can live free from fear and prejudice, and fully realize their potential as equal contributors to a just, inclusive, and progressive society.

#AreaNews: National Youth Commission (NYC) Baguio Attends the 1st Quarter Regional Development Council in CAR
 
The Regional Development Council (RDC) of the Cordillera Administrative Region recently held its 1st Quarter Meeting, marked by key recognitions and notable discussions on regional concerns. The meeting took place at the Provincial Capitol in Baguio City and brought together prominent figures, including Governor Elias Bulut and Ms. Edna Tabanda.

One of the event's highlights was the posthumous recognition of Ms. Pura Sumangil for her significant contributions to the infrastructure committee. This heartfelt acknowledgment underscored her lasting impact on the region's development.

Outstanding volunteers were also honored during the meeting. Awardees included Jomar Herry, Atty. Nerissa Bayan, and the Tanggew Community Library, each recognized for their dedication and positive influence in their respective fields.

The City Government of Baguio and the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) received the prestigious Philippine Quality Awards (PQA), personally accepted by Mayor Benjamin Magalong and Regional Director Araceli San Jose. The PQA is a prestigious recognition for organizations excelling in leadership, strategy, customer results, and overall organizational performance, modeled after the Baldrige Excellence Framework.

Baguio Cityâ€™s recognition reflects its improved urban management strategies, sustainability initiatives, and efficient public service delivery. Meanwhile, the DILG's award signifies its commitment to strengthening local governance, promoting transparency, and driving innovation in public service.

The council also tackled pressing legislative matters, approving 17 of the 18 agenda items. However, the proposed Senate Bill 1979, known as the "Prevention of Adolescent Pregnancy Act of 2025," faced disapproval due to concerns raised by members. The bill, designed to curb the rising rates of adolescent pregnancies in the country, sparked debates surrounding comprehensive sexual education, access to reproductive health services, and age-related parental consent provisions.

The meeting concluded at 12:00 PM, closing a productive session dedicated to recognizing achievements and addressing regional challenges.

#AreaNews | NYC Baguio Facilitates Bridging Leadership Training for Youth Leaders

The National Youth Commission (NYC) Baguio Area Office successfully conducted a comprehensive leadership training workshop focused on bridging leadership for Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) officials, Local Youth Development Council (LYDC) members, and various youth organizations. The event, held in Calasiao, Pangasinan, aimed to equip youth leaders with essential skills to effectively serve their communities and contribute to national development.

The training commenced with a solemn opening ceremony, which included a prayer, the National Anthem, and the Pangasinan Hymn. SK President of Calasiao, Narayana Rsi Das Mesina, delivered the welcome and inspirational messages. He underscored the critical importance of annual leadership training in strengthening youth leadership capabilities and fostering their active engagement in municipal youth development initiatives. Mr. Mesina encouraged participants to fully leverage the training, reflect on their significant roles, and embrace the responsibilities inherent in their community leadership positions.

This seminar was firmly grounded in Republic Act No. 8044, also known as the Youth in Nation-Building Act of 1995. This foundational legislation mandates the NYC to lead in the formulation of youth-related policies and programs, thereby guiding youth development efforts at the national level. The Act specifically empowers the NYC to enhance the leadership skills of the youth, foster nationalism and patriotism, and integrate Filipino culture and values into nation-building endeavors.

Mr. Dave Homer Ariola and Ms. Sheridan Athena Gajete from the National Youth Commission Baguio Area Office provided technical assistance throughout the training. Their expertise ensured that all participants gained a comprehensive understanding of effective youth leadership and governance principles.
The workshop was strategically designed to build a robust foundation in leadership for grassroots youth leaders.

A central theme of the seminar was bridging leadership, a critical skill emphasized for SK and LYDC members. This concept focuses on connecting disparate groups and stakeholders to address common challenges and achieve shared objectives, effectively bridging the gap between youth and local governance.

Furthermore, Mr. Dave Homer Ariola introduced the SK Web Portal and Learning Management System (LMS). These digital platforms are designed to streamline the management of SK activities, enhance accessibility to resources, provide training materials, and improve communication channels. Ms. Sheridan Athena Gajete also provided the latest Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) updates, ensuring leaders remained informed about recent initiatives, programs, and policies affecting the youth sector.

The interactive nature of the seminar, featuring group discussions, project development exercises, and coaching sessions, allowed participants to apply newly acquired skills in practical scenarios and collaboratively brainstorm solutions to challenges prevalent in their respective barangays.

The National Youth Commission Baguio Area Office remains committed to empowering Filipino youth, equipping them with the knowledge and skills necessary to become effective leaders and active contributors to national progress.
