#AreaNews | NYC Baguio Area Office Holds Online Orientation on LYDO Accreditation for SK Mandatory Training

The National Youth Commission (NYC) Baguio Area Office recently conducted an online orientation regarding the accreditation process for Local Youth Development Offices (LYDOs). This session is a crucial step for the upcoming Sangguniang Kabataan Mandatory Training (SKMT) program.

The accreditation covers the entire Mandatory Training Program and any other modules approved by the Commission. Applicants will receive access to training modules and facilitators' guides, from which they must select one preferred topic. 

Accredited LYDOs will gain the opportunity to collaborate with reputable learning institutions, including Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) or organizations affiliated with State Universities and Colleges, for conducting the Mandatory Training programs.

The online orientation saw active participation of thirty-two (32) LYDO representatives from Region 1 and the Cordillera Administrative Region (CAR). The session served as an invaluable platform for LYDOs to raise inquiries and gain clear clarifications on the accreditation process.

#AreaNews | Balaoan Youth Embrace Collective Action for a Safer, Drug-Free Community

Young leaders in Balaoan recently came together for WEMBOREE, an event focused on empowering them to build a safer and drug-free community. The goal was simple: shift their thinking from "Me" (individual) to "We" (collective), encouraging them to actively participate and partner for a better Balaoan.

Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) Officials from all 36 barangays attended WEMBOREE. The event aimed to get youth more involved, strengthen partnerships, teach them about disaster preparedness, and raise awareness about drug prevention.

SKPP Patricia Mae Suyat and LYDO Staff Kevin Occasion delivered inspiring messages, while MLGOO Ms. Marife Doculan spoke about the crucial role of youth in Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM).
A key part of WEMBOREE was changing the mindset from "Me" to "We." Through group discussions, participants explored how working together could benefit their communities, especially in handling disasters and preventing drug abuse.

Ms. Sheridan Athena Gajete from the National Youth Commission (NYC) led team-building activities to boost cooperation and leadership. She also taught the youth about their vital role in DRRM, emphasizing they can be first responders and help with emergency planning and community awareness.
A significant outcome was the creation of action plans. In workshops, participants developed strategies to improve their barangays' disaster response and create anti-drug campaigns, focusing on education and healthy lifestyles.

The event also stressed the importance of community partnerships. Youth leaders were encouraged to collaborate with their peers, local government, and other organizations to drive community initiatives.
"WEMBOREE successfully gave our youth the skills and knowledge to actively participate in disaster reduction and drug prevention," said a local official. "The shift from 'Me' to 'We' was evident, and we believe these empowered youth will be crucial in creating a safer, healthier Balaoan."

The event ended with a ceremony where participants shared their commitments to apply what they learned. They pledged to work together for a more resilient and drug-free Balaoan, receiving certificates to mark their dedication.

WEMBOREE laid a strong foundation for a more resilient and drug-free community by engaging youth, fostering collaboration, and promoting a shared vision.

#AreaNews | NYC Baguio Joins Cordillera Regional Gender and Development Committee (RGADC) for 2025 National Women's Month Opening

The National Youth Commission (NYC) Baguio joined the Cordillera Regional Gender and Development Committee (RGADC-CAR) today for the launch of the 2025 National Women's Month Celebration. The event, held at the DSWD FO CAR Training Center, brought together various groups, including academics and non-profits, to start a month of activities promoting women's empowerment.

DSWD FO CAR Regional Director Maria A. Catbagan-Aplaten, who chairs RGADC-CAR, led a discussion with experts and stakeholders. They answered questions from the media and public, both in person and online.
Director Aplaten highlighted DSWD programs that support women and committed to ongoing collaboration during Women's Month. This year's theme is "Babae sa Lahat ng Sektor, Aangat ang Bukas sa Bagong Pilipinas" (Women in all sectors, tomorrow will rise in the New Philippines). She also shared the month's activity calendar and introduced the new RGADC logo.

Panelists included Dr. Ferdinand P. Gonzales, RGADC-CAR Co-Chair; Edna C. Tabanda, Regional Development Council Co-Chair; Rosella Camte-Banhi, Regional Pool of GAD Resource Person; and Atty. Lyndon T. Morales from the Commission on Human Rights - CAR.

Dr. Gonzales encouraged local governments and agencies to have their staff join the GAD (Gender and Development) resource pool. This group, formed by the Philippine Commission on Women, provides expertise, conducts research, and helps monitor GAD efforts. There are currently eight members in the national GAD resource pool and three regionally.

The event also featured the unveiling of the RGADC-CAR logo. Its design, a circle with eight colors, represents the six provinces and two cities of the Cordillera, united. Gender symbols and figures of a man and woman stand for gender equality. The Cordillera map signifies regional identity, and the color purple symbolizes solidarity with women's empowerment.

NYC Baguio is eager to participate in this month-long celebration, supporting initiatives that advance gender equality and empower women throughout the Cordillera Administrative Region.

#AreaNews | La Unionâ€™s Commitment to Quality Youth Services
 
The Provincial Government of La Union (PGLU), in partnership with key agencies, successfully carried out an onsite monitoring and evaluation (M&E) of youth programs across all Local Government Units (LGUs) in the province. This collaborative effort, led by the Provincial Social Welfare and Development Office (PSWDO), focused on assessing the performance and compliance of youth services with the Seal of Good Local Governance (SGLG) criteria.
 
The M&E process aimed to ensure that youth programs align with governance standards, emphasizing transparency, accountability, and effective service delivery. The evaluation included reviewing crucial documents such as the Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) M&E Forms, Local Youth Development Plans (LYDP), SK Accomplishment Reports, and minutes of the Quarterly City Youth Development Council (CYDC) meetings. These documents provided valuable insights into the effectiveness of local youth programs and their alignment with the SGLG's rigorous standards.
 
The monitoring team, composed of representatives from the National Youth Commission (NYC), Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG), Panlalawigang Pederasyon ng mga Sangguniang Kabataan (PPSK), and the Provincial Youth Development Council (PYDC), worked together to assess governance and youth development processes. The feedback provided during the M&E will serve as a foundation for improving youth services, ensuring that LGUs continue to foster youth empowerment, promote active youth participation in governance, and address the needs of the youth population across La Union.
This event marks another step toward strengthening youth governance and development, setting a solid foundation for more effective youth programs and services in the future. It highlights the commitment of La Union to deliver quality youth services that adhere to the highest standards of transparency and accountability.

#AreaNews | Strengthening Youth Governance: NYC Naga Area Office Engages SK Leaders in Malilipot Dialogue

In a proactive move to fortify the leadership and accountability of the Sangguniang Kabataan (SK), the National Youth Commission (NYC), together with local officials, held a meaningful dialogue with SK leaders at the Municipal Hall of Malilipot, Albay.The meeting, facilitated by Youth Development Officer Justin Bardon, aimed to address pressing issues confronting SK councils, especially in the areas of financial management, project implementation, and administrative compliance.

SK Federation President Hon. Christian Balingbina opened the session by encouraging youth leaders to actively participate, followed by Hon. Avelino Binalla, LIGA President, who emphasized the importance of financial responsibility among SK officials.Ms. Delfin led the core dialogue, highlighting key provisions of the SK Reform Laws (RA 10742 and RA 11768) and clarifying the functions of the SK. The conversation surfaced several concerns: non-submission of reports, inactive officials, unutilized budgets, and lack of knowledge on required financial templates.

Constructive recommendations emerged from the discussion, including tapping barangay bookkeepers for assistance, coordinating with accounting units, adopting a buddy system for financial report preparation, and passing resolutions for COA-led trainings.

NYC also urged SK councils to align their programs with the Ten Centers of Participation of the Philippine Youth Development Plan and to conduct comprehensive KK profiling as a foundation for relevant youth programs.The dialogue concluded with a strong call to action from Mr. Bardon, who reminded all participants of their shared responsibility in supporting and elevating youth governance at the grassroots level.

ðŸ“¢ Call for Submissions | Second iDiaspora Photo Contest 2025 ðŸ“¸

Join the global movement to highlight and honor the contributions of diaspora communities. Together, we can inspire others to recognize the power of migration!

In 2022, the International Organization for Migration (IOM) IOM Asia-Pacific and IBER-RUTAS launched the First iDiaspora Photo Contest, celebrating cultural diversity and showcasing the profound socio-cultural contributions of diaspora communities worldwide. With 93 stunning entries from over 39 countries, the contest sparked global conversations about identity, heritage, and the power of community support during times of crisis. 

Inspired by this success, the IOM and IBER-RUTAS are thrilled to announce the Second iDiaspora Photo Contest, set to launch in 2025. Open to professional and amateur photographers alike, this contest invites participants to capture the vibrant stories of diaspora and migrant communities and their vital role in shaping a better future. 

Participants are invited to submit a series of three photographs that capture diaspora contributions in one or more of the following categories: 
 
(1) Health and Well-being: Showcase how diaspora communities improve health outcomes and foster well-being in their host and home countries.
 
(2) Climate Action and Environment: Highlight the innovative ways diaspora communities address climate change and promote environmental sustainability.
 
(3) Gender Equality and Youth Empowerment: Capturing how diaspora communities advocate for women's empowerment and inspire the next generation of leaders.

The iDiaspora Photo Contest will have two categories: Amateur and professional. 

To apply for the contest: 
1. Check if you are between 18 â€“ 32 years of age, and you are neither the IOM and/or IBER-RUTAS staff nor their immediate family (spouse, mother, father, sons, daughters) or those who are part of their household. 
2. Register in iDiaspora.org.
3. Complete the application form where you must include: 
(1) Personal data 
(2) Select the categories for your series. While there is no limit to the number of photos in each selected category, only three photos should be submitted.  
(3) Title of the photographic series. 
(4) Brief description of the series, containing at least the motivations and the relevance to the theme.
(5) Submission of the 3 selected photos. 
 
Registration is free and open until 22nd May 2025 at 15:00 hours in Geneva, Switzerland.

At the end of the registration period, the photographic series will be evaluated by a panel of expert juries in order to determine the winning entries and special mentions. 

Winners will be announced approximately 15 days after the closure of the contest on the iDiaspora and IBER-RUTAS websites. The winning participants will also be notified via the email addresses provided during registration. 
 
The prizes for amateur and professional categories will be: 
First Prize USD $1,000.00 (One Thousand United States dollars). 
Second Prize USD $700.00 (Seven Hundred United States dollars). 
Third Prize USD $300.00 (Three Hundred United States dollars).

ICYMI | NYC HIGHLIGHTS YOUTH INNOVATION AT BSPâ€™S YOUTH FINANCIAL INCLUSION CONGRESS

The National Youth Commission (NYC) participated in the Youth Financial Inclusion (YFI) Congress held last April 2, 2025, themed â€œEmpowering the Next Generation of Financially Included Filipinos.â€ The event, which brought together students and young leaders from across the Philippines at the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) Head Office in Manila, served as an avenue to engage the Filipino youth in discussions and initiatives focused on boosting financial literacy and access to financial services.A highlight of the congress was the YFI Hackathon, a competition where student teams from various universities showcased their creativity in promoting financial inclusion. They presented innovative proposals and community-based solutions leveraging digitalization to advance financial inclusion nationwide. 

NYC Chairperson and CEO, USec. Joseph Francisco â€œJeffâ€ R. Ortega played a key role as a judge, evaluating the groundbreaking applications and proposals presented. USec. Ortega delivered an inspiring message, highlighting the crucial role of the youth in shaping the nation's future. He emphasized the importance of their unique perspectives in identifying and proposing innovative solutions to the country's challenges, particularly in financial inclusion. He also commended the students and youth participants for their dedication, evident in their proposals aimed at fostering financial inclusion within their communities.

The â€œAniKansyaâ€ team from the Polytechnic University of the Philippines (PUP) Manila was the winning proposal for the YFI Hackathon, which outlined a digital platform designed to connect Filipino farmers with a range of essential financial products and services. The team behind â€œAniKansyaâ€ comprised Caitlin Faith Hashimoto, Marjorie Cabato, and Bart Patrick Familar. 

Other notable hackathon awardees included:
-Second place: De La Salle Universityâ€™s Cyber Archers team with â€œPURSE,â€ a platform offering accessible financial services, receipt organization software, and digital cards tailored for persons with disabilities. The team members were Daphne Loo, John Ray Cardeno, Shaina Julao, and Shekinah Marquez. 

-Third place: Team Patch from Ateneo de Manila University and the University of the Philippines with â€œSMS Fraud Checker,â€ an AI-powered feature designed to detect and warn users of potential scam texts in real-time. The team consisted of Azzedine Abrencillo, Gabrielle Dotado, Jan Llenzl Dagohoy, and Angelica Marie Casuela.

-Fourth place: Junquera Tech Solutions from the University of San Carlos with â€œMigoPay,â€ a solar-powered, offline-capable fintech solution utilizing contactless tap-to-pay for Cebuâ€™s fisherfolk. The team members were Juliana Marie Mabugat, Kameron Zoe Cameron, Rose Ann Gesta, Katrina Bianca Tacandong, and Janica Marie Ycong.

-Fifth place: JVFC Malaya from PUP Manila with â€œTeknOffline,â€ a near-field communication-enabled e-wallet facilitating digital transactions in areas with limited or no internet access. The team included Jayson Santiago, Jose Manuel Lapesora, Csean Chloe Olleta, Frederick Gabriel Ramos, and Veniz Velasquez. 

Furthermore, USec. Jeff Ortega spearheaded the awarding ceremony for the inaugural cohort of YFI Ambassadors. The ambassadors, representing Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao, will champion the cause of youth and financial inclusion at the grassroots level. They will act as leaders in raising awareness and driving action within their respective communities through action plans focused on initiatives like promoting digital payments on campuses, conducting financial literacy seminars, and developing student-centric financial management apps.

For more information, visit the Financial Inclusion Steering Committee (FISC) website at https://financialinclusion.gov.ph/.

PRESS RELEASE | Safe Spaces, Stronger Youth: NYC Pushes for Child Protection Policies in All Registered Youth Organizations

In a proactive step toward safeguarding the rights and welfare of Filipino children and youth, the National Youth Commission (NYC) has officially approved a resolution encouraging all Youth Organization Registration Program (YORP) members to develop their own Child Protection Policy.

The resolution, sponsored by the Commissioner,  ASec. Mae Mae Gonzales  and adopted by the Commission Proper through Ad Referendum on April 11, 2025, seeks to strengthen the protection of children against all forms of abuse, especially within the spaces where youth-led activities are carried out.

Anchored on Section 13, Article II of the 1987 Philippine Constitution and aligned with several landmark legislationsâ€”including Republic Act No. 8044 (Youth in Nation-Building Act), RA 7610, and the newly enacted RA 11930 (Anti-OSAEC and CSAEM Act)â€”the resolution emphasizes the need for grassroots-level action.

The NYC also underscored alarming data from the Situation of Children in the Philippines, 2024, which revealed a staggering 264.6% increase in online sexual abuse and exploitation of children between 2019 and 2020. This trend amplifies the urgency of equipping youth organizations with policies and tools to respond effectively.

With thousands of youth organizations registered under YORP, this directive is expected to catalyze a nationwide movement toward safer and more responsible youth-led initiatives. Beyond compliance, YORP-registered organizations that develop and implement their own child protection policies will be better positioned to access more capacity-building opportunities, partnerships, and government support.

This initiative is part of NYCâ€™s broader commitment under the Philippine Youth Development Plan (PYDP) 2023â€“2028, particularly in the areas of Social Inclusion and Equity.

Letâ€™s build a nation where every youth-led space is a safe space.

Participants explored self-awareness, partnership building, and youth project development, essential skills for leading the Katipunan ng Kabataan at the local level.

Key topics included alignment of personal ownership and participation, coaching and mentoring, collaboration and multi-sector participation, bridging leadership, discussion on SK Web Portal, Learning Management System, and other updates, and adolescent pregnancy awareness.

The training, mandated by Republic Act (RA) No. 8044, the Youth in Nation-Building Act of 1995, aims to enhance the skills and leadership potential of the youth, instilling in them nationalism, patriotism, and Filipino cultural values.

One of the highlights of this productive meeting was the completion of the Adolescent Mother Profiling Tool, designed to gather data and assess the needs and challenges of adolescent mothers for targeted interventions, support programs, and services.

Additionally, an orientation was conducted on the process and standards for accrediting healthcare facilities that provide adolescent-friendly services, including reproductive health, mental health, and general wellness programs for adolescents.

Furthermore, the inclusion of the Family Planning Organization of the Philippines as the councilâ€™s main representative for youth concerns on reproductive health, family planning, and youth empowerment was also highlighted.

With all the critical concerns presented during the meeting, NYC laid out plans for more proactive engagement and collaborative efforts dedicated to promoting comprehensive and responsive strategies to address the unique health challenges faced by young people.

PRESS RELEASE | NYC COMMISSIONER-AT-LARGE ASEC. KARL LEGAZPI ON THE RECENT REPORT FROM CHINA DAILY

FREE OUR STUDENTS: As a fellow PalaweÃ±o and Commissioner-at-Large of the National Youth Commission, I am deeply alarmed and outraged by the recent report from China Daily, an official mouthpiece of the Chinese Communist Party, which publicly accused three young PalaweÃ±osâ€”David ServaÃ±ez, Albert Endencia, and Nathalie Plizardoâ€”of espionage.

These individualsâ€”reportedly former scholars of the Palawan provincial governmentâ€”are now detained in China under grave, opaque charges that appear to lack credible evidence, transparency, or due process. They were named and shamed in state-controlled media, with no clear legal proceedings or access to proper representation.

It is unfortunate, and at the same time ironic, that these kababayans of ours, who are now legally staying and working in China, went there originally as scholars - as part of efforts to deepen people-to-people relations and promote mutual  understanding between the Province of Palawan and Philippines and the Chinese province of Hainan.

This is a blatant act of propaganda and intimidation. To use state-controlled media to parade young Filipinos as alleged spies is not only irresponsibleâ€”it is cruel and inhumane. These are not criminals; they are youth who pursued international opportunities to grow, learn, and serve their homeland.

As a government official, I firmly believe that this incident deserves urgent and coordinated action. I respectfully urge the Department of Foreign Affairs and all relevant agencies to exhaust all diplomatic channels to secure access to our detained kababayans, verify their condition, and ensure their rights are upheld under international law.

This incident must also prompt a broader conversation about the safety of our young Filipinos abroadâ€”particularly in countries where political systems can turn vulnerable individuals into pawns in broader geopolitical tensions. We must build stronger safeguards and more vigilant support systems for our youth working or studying overseas.

I stand in solidarity with the families of David, Albert, and Nathalie, and with every young Filipino who deserves to be treated with dignity and fairnessâ€”no matter where in the world they may be.

As National Youth Commissioner, I will work to ensure that this issue remains part of the national youth agenda. The Filipino youth are not pawns. They are the future of our nationâ€”and they deserve nothing less than justice, protection, and truth.

Comm Karl Legazpi

The Philippine Coast Guard, Philippine Information Agency, and National Youth Commission successfully held the West Philippine Sea (WPS) Youth Forum, bringing together over 500 students from the Polytechnic University of the Philippines in a unified stand to defend the future of the West Philippine Sea.

The U.S. Department of State's "State Magazine" (statemag.state.gov) has featured a significant visit by officials from the National Youth Commission (NYC) to the U.S. Embassy in Manila. The article showcases the productive dialogue between U.S. Ambassador to the Philippines, MaryKay L. Carlson, and the NYC delegation.

The feature highlights a key moment where Ambassador Carlson engaged with the NYC members, using visual aids to illustrate the complex dynamics of the South China Sea. Specifically, the article notes, "U.S. Ambassador to the Philippines MaryKay L. Carlson discusses South China Sea policy while looking at a map of the region and a model of the Peopleâ€™s Republic of China (PRC) military installation at Fiery Cross Reefâ€”a disputed atoll claimed by the Philippines, PRC, Taiwan, and Vietnamâ€”with National Youth Commission of the Philippines members in her office, Jan. 14."
 
NYC Chairperson and Chief Executive Officer USec. Jeff Ortega led the delegation, focusing on exploring potential collaborations, particularly through initiatives like the Young Southeast Asian Leaders Initiative (YSEALI). The discussions underscored the importance of empowering young leaders and fostering regional cooperation.

The article also emphasizes the vital role of the NYC in providing policy direction to the Sangguniang Kabataan (SK), the local youth councils across the Philippines. These councils, composed of elected members aged 18 to 24 from each barangay (municipal area), serve as crucial platforms for youth engagement in local governance.

The publication further notes the U.S. Embassy's commitment to engaging with these young leaders, recognizing the SK as a significant stepping stone for future political careers. "The U.S. Embassy in Manila engages with these youth leaders early and often because the SK is often the first rung of a long political career," the article states.

PRESS RELEASE | NYC Approves Resolution Strengthening Youth Support Against Adolescent Pregnancy and HIV

The National Youth Commission (NYC) has approved its official position on Senate Resolution No. 13, which addresses the critical issues of adolescent pregnancy and HIV infections among Filipino youth.  This action, under Policy Resolution No. 2, Series of 2025, underscores the NYC's commitment to prioritizing the health and well-being of the country's young population.    

The resolution, sponsored by Commissioner Bianca Patrice C. Go, supports the strengthening of Comprehensive Sexuality Education (CSE), enhanced access to youth-friendly reproductive health services, and the active involvement of youth in policy development.  This proactive stance aims to combat the rising challenges posed by adolescent pregnancy and HIV/AIDS, aligning with the Philippine Youth Development Plan (PYDP) 2023-2028.    

The approved position paper will be formally transmitted to relevant government bodies, including the Senate and executive agencies, to inform and influence legislative and policy discussions.  The NYC's firm stance reflects its mandate to serve as the primary policy-making and coordinating body on youth-related concerns, as outlined in Republic Act No. 8044, also known as the Youth in Nation-Building Act.

LATEST | NYC, CPD, NAPC-YSSC COLLABORATE IN UPDATING TRAINING MODULE FOR ASRH 

The National Youth Commission (NYC), Commission on Population and Development (CPD) and the National Anti-Poverty Commission (NAPC)-Youth and Students Sectoral Council (YSSC) convened in a meeting to discuss the development of Teenage Pregnancy and Adolescent Sexual and Reproductive Health (ASRH) Toolkit, as well as the updating of the existing module on promoting youth participation in the formulation of gender-responsive and life-skills based local youth policies.

Key recommendations emerged from the meeting, including the integration of ASRH into Local Youth Development Plans (LYDPs) through a structured planning process. During the meeting, it was also emphasized to conduct consultations and mapping of resources with key stakeholders, such as the Sangguniang Kabataan (SK), Local Youth Development Councils (LYDC), and Local Youth Development Offices (LYDO).

Furthermore, the meeting addressed the updating and expanding of the existing module to include the planning cycle, communication and advocacy planning, and monitoring and evaluation strategies.

#AreaNews | National Youth Commission Zamboanga Area Office Conducts Comprehensive Monitoring Visits of SK Officials in Zamboanga City

The National Youth Commission (NYC) Zamboanga Area Office successfully completed monitoring visits across all 98 barangays of Zamboanga City last February 1-15, 2025. The visits, conducted in clustered groups, aimed to support and assess the compliance of Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) officials with Republic Act 10742, also known as the SK Reform Act.

The "Kumustahan and Monitoring" visits were facilitated in venues designated by the Office of the SK Federation President, ensuring accessibility and efficiency. It also aims to provide on-the-ground assistance and continuous guidance to SK officials throughout their tenure.

The NYC Zamboanga Area Office focused on key compliance areas, including conduct of monthly meetings to ensure regular and productive sessions for SK operations, Katipunan ng Kabataan Assemblies to verify the execution of youth assemblies for participatory governance, financial Compliance by reviewing adherence to financial regulations and proper fund management, full disclosure or transparency boards, and submission of key planning and budgeting documents.

NYC Zamboanga also emphasized the importance of direct interaction with SK officials in their barangays for effective monitoring and evaluation and that these visits also aim to address gaps by providing practical support beyond the initial mandatory training sessions.

#AreaNews | Strengthening Youth Leadership and Governance in Ilocos Norte

The National Youth Commission (NYC) Baguio Area Office provided technical assistance during the First Quarter Local Youth Development Council Meeting in Ilocos Norte, fostering collaboration and enhancing youth governance. NYC representatives Dr. Sheridan Athena Gajete and Mr. Dave Homer Ariola, alongside the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) Ilocos Norte, engaged with Local Youth Development Officers (LYDOs) to discuss key policies and programs.

Discussions centered on SK updates, the provisions of JMC DILG-NYC 2024-001, and the Philippine Youth Development Plan (PYDP) 2023-2028. NYC also presented the SK Web Portal, compliance templates, and monitoring and evaluation of youth programs. Notably, the draft Constitution and By-Laws for the institutionalization of the Association of Local Youth Development Officers of Ilocos Norte were finalized, ensuring a more unified and effective youth leadership network.

Clarifications on the appointment of SK Officers-in-Charge (OICs), the need for clear guidelines on honoraria, and procedural concerns on filling SK vacancies were also discussed with NYC and DILG for legal assessment and policy refinement.

The meeting concluded with a renewed commitment to strengthening youth participation and governance, ensuring that young leaders in Ilocos Norte are equipped to drive meaningful change in their communities.

#AreaNews | NYC Joins in Advancing Youth Leadership and Strengthening Governance in La Union

The National Youth Commission (NYC) Baguio Area Office extended technical assistance during the First Quarter Local Youth Development Officer Meeting in La Union, reinforcing efforts to enhance youth governance and leadership. Represented by  Dr. Sheridan Athena Gajete, NYC engaged with Local Youth Development Officers (LYDOs) and DILG La Union representative Ms. Charina Flora to discuss vital policies and programs aimed at strengthening youth participation.

Key discussions focused on updates regarding the Sangguniang Kabataan (SK), the provisions of JMC DILG-NYC 2024-001, and the institutionalization of the Association of Local Youth Development Officers of La Union. NYC also addressed concerns surrounding the SK vacancies and the implementation of the SK Special Elections (SKSE), with LYDOs urging for its immediate prioritization to ensure active youth representation in barangays.

A milestone of the meeting was the drafting of the Constitution and By-Laws for the League of Local Youth Development Officers of La Union, facilitated by PYDO, Ms. Zyjazz de Guzman. The election of officers was successfully conducted, with Dr. Jerome G. Acupan of the City of San Fernando emerging as the newly elected President.

All queries and concerns raised, particularly regarding SKSE and the role of the Commission on Elections (COMELEC) in ensuring youth participation, have been forwarded to the appropriate NYC and DILG offices for further action. The meeting concluded with a collective commitment to building a stronger, more inclusive youth governance framework in La Union.

Through the National Youth Commission Philippines, the Singapore Ministry of Education opened the application for the 2026 ASEAN Scholarship which will provide pre-tertiary study support for students from the Philippines.

The scholarship is open to all Filipino students in Grade 9 and above. This programme will empower students to access a world-class and holistic education, giving them exposure to global opportunities. As a fellow ASEAN member country, Singapore is committed to working with the Philippines to nurture and develop the leaders of tomorrow. This programme will forge strong bonds and promote goodwill among our young generation.

Requirements:

#NatCon2025 DAY 1 RECAP | NYC CONCLUDES SUCCESSFUL FIRST DAY OF 2025 NATIONAL YOUTH CONVENTION

The National Youth Commission (NYC) successfully concluded the first day of its 2025 National Youth Convention: Youth Development Convention and Policy Forum, Alab: Empowering the Bagong Pilipinas Generation. The event aims to gather Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) officials, Local Youth Development Officers (LYDOs), and members of Local Youth Development Councils (LYDCs) from across the nation.

SK National Representative Commissioner CJ Dalipe delivered her opening remarks, highlighting the importance of policy consulation for SK and updates on upcoming NYC projects aimed at amplifying youth engagement and capacity-building efforts. Atty. James Ceasar Ventura, Chief of the Regional Youth Development Division, provided a comprehensive rationale and overview of the convention while Dr. Sheridan Athena Gajete of the NYC Baguio Area Office, led an engaging expectation-setting session, ensuring participant alignment and maximizing the convention's impact.

#AreaNews | Bukidnon LYDOs and SK Unite for Strategic Action

The Joint SK Pederasyon Presidents and the Bukidnon League of Local Youth Development Officers recently held their First Quarter Meeting and Target Settingâ€”marking a key step toward stronger and more effective youth governance. The gathering provided a platform for youth leaders to align their goals, refine strategies, and strengthen collaboration for the year.

Participants gained valuable insights into the SK Web Portal, monitoring and evaluation, bookkeeping, and mandatory training facilitated by the National Youth Commission. These tools are essential for enhancing transparency and efficiency in youth governance. A reorientation led by DILG Bukidnon Provincial Director Rhona Abadilla further clarified the roles and responsibilities of LYDOs and SK Pederasyon Presidents, ensuring each leader is equipped to maximize their impact.

A major highlight of the event was the discussion on 2025 programs and initiatives, where youth leaders exchanged ideas for creating meaningful and responsive projects for their communities. The election of new officers for the Bukidnon League of LYDOs also marked a fresh start for leadership and representation, reinforcing the provinceâ€™s commitment to youth development.

This meeting reaffirmed the dedication of SK leaders, LYDOs, and partner agencies in fostering proactive and innovative youth leadership. With a shared vision and renewed commitment, Bukidnonâ€™s youth sector is set to achieve greater milestones this year.

#AreaNews | Elevating Leadership: Moderatorâ€™s Summit Inspires Youth to Drive Sustainable Change

The Moderatorâ€™s Summit x Movers brought together 200 dynamic student leaders and moderators from various academic and non-academic organizations of the University of Nueva Caceres for a transformative capacity-building experience. Organized by the University Student Government, the summit ignited collaboration, leadership development, and a deeper commitment to sustainability among young changemakers.

The National Youth Commission-Naga Area Office, represented by Mr. Benito Lot Jr. O. Reyes, delivered a session titled "Beyond Borders, Beyond Challenges." The discussion emphasized youth leadership in advancing the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and integrating the 2023â€“2028 Philippine Youth Development Plan (PYDP) into local initiatives. Participants gained insights into the interconnection of global and national frameworks, heard inspiring success stories of youth-led sustainability efforts, and explored actionable strategies to align their organizationsâ€™ programs with meaningful impact.

The summit also featured other speakers, including Ms. Nikayla Karell Odi, who guided students in designing sustainable and forward-thinking initiatives, and Atty. Maria Leonor Gerona Robredo, who engaged participants in an insightful dialogue on leadership and transformative action.

The event fostered active discussions, bold ideas, and renewed commitments among student leaders. As NYC continues to strengthen partnerships with academic institutions, the summit reinforced the power of youth-driven solutions in shaping a more sustainable and progressive future.

#AreaNews | Advancing Youth Governance: Surigao City LYDO Strengthens Strategies Through Benchmarking

The Local Youth Development Office (LYDO) of Surigao City, together with the SK Pederasyon President and staff, took a proactive step in enhancing youth participation through a benchmarking activity at the National Youth Commission (NYC)-Cagayan de Oro Area Office. This initiative aimed to gather best practices and insights to refine youth governance and development efforts.

The participants engaged in discussions on key topics, including the Philippine Youth Development Plan (PYDP) 2023-2028, monitoring and evaluation processes, and the Youth Organization Registration Program (YORP). The importance of timely data submission and integrating youth organizations into the Local Youth Development Council (LYDC) was also emphasized, providing valuable strategies for strengthening local programs.

LYDO representatives also raised challenges that may affect program implementation and monitoring, leading to discussions on ways to streamline YORP accreditation and improve coordination between local organizations and the city government.

This benchmarking activity served as a valuable learning experience, equipping Surigao City's youth leaders with actionable strategies to enhance governance and community engagement. Committed to applying their newfound knowledge, both LYDO Surigao and NYC-CDO pledged to sustain their collaboration in empowering young leaders and addressing local concerns effectively.

#ForTheFilipinoYouth #ParaSaKabataangPilipino

#AreaNews | Strengthening Youth Engagement: Dinagat Islands and NYC-CDO Forge Stronger Ties

The Population Office of the Province of Dinagat Islands (PDI) and the Local Youth Development Officer II (LYDO II) recently visited the National Youth Commission (NYC)-CDO Area Office to strengthen collaboration and enhance youth programs.

During the visit, the PDI team shared their progress in organizing youth groups from program beneficiaries and explored ways to connect them with LYDOs while facilitating their registration under the Youth Organization Registration Program (YORP). With guidance from the NYC-CDO Area Office, these efforts aim to formalize and strengthen youth participation in governance.

Discussions centered on the roles of SK, LYDOs, LYDCs, and LGUs in youth development, highlighting the importance of synergy in promoting leadership and engagement. The team also examined best practices from other LGUs, learned about compliance tools like the SK Web Portal, and gained insights into monitoring and evaluation processes. Benchmarking visits to the Oro Youth Development Office and the City Population Office provided additional strategies to improve their initiatives.

This partnership underscores a shared commitment to equipping young leaders with the resources and networks they need to drive meaningful change in their communities.

#AreaNews | Strengthening Action Against Child Labor: CLEAR CAR Unites Stakeholders for Lasting Change

The National Youth Commission (NYC)- Baguio Area Office recently attended the launch of the Child Labor-Effective Awareness and Response in the Cordillera Administrative Region (CLEAR CAR) Project by World Vision. The initiative, co-funded by the European Union and supported by numerous international and local partners, takes a significant stride forward in combating child labor. Its objective is to eliminate the most severe forms of child labor, particularly Online Sexual Abuse or Exploitation of Children (OSAEC) and violations of acceptable working conditions in the Philippines.

Bringing together key stakeholders, including local government units from Benguet, Mountain Province, and Ifugao, as well as child-focused agencies, the event strengthened commitments to safeguard and empower vulnerable children. The discussions focused on formalizing partnerships, sharing preliminary study findings, and evaluating the projectâ€™s initial achievements to ensure its long-term impqct.

The NYC, reaffirmed its commitment alongside representatives from the European Union, LGUs, academe, and youth leaders, all pledging to take decisive action against child labor.

With inspiring messages from regional leaders and the collective resolve of partners, the launch marks a turning point in strengthening child protection mechanisms in the region. As the event concluded, a powerful call to action resonatedâ€”one of unity, responsibility, and relentless advocacy for a future free from child labor.

Hon. Doculan, MLGOO of Balaoan, discussed vital DILG issuances, ensuring participants understood reporting and compliance requirements, with LYDO staff Ashley Ariel Palabay emphasizing the submission of M&E forms for SGLG compliance.

Dr. Sheridan Athena Gajete of the NYC Baguio Area Office delivered an inspiring discussion on leadership and the LMS and SK Web Portal, emphasizing its role in equipping SK, LYDO, and LYDC members with tools for effective communication, efficient reporting, and continuous growth. Call for Applications: HOST FAMILIES for JENESYS2024 INBOUND PROGRAM The Philippines-Japan Development Exchange (Dispatch of Japanese University Students)

IMPORTANT REMINDERS:
â€¢ Online submissions only (via google form)
â€¢ Please carefully read the instructions on how to apply. 

Through the National Youth Commission (NYC) and Japan International Cooperation Center (JICE) will organize the JENESYS2024 The Philippines-Japan Development Exchange (Dispatch of Japanese University Students) on February 26-March 3, 2025 in Manila, Philippines. 
The JENESYS (Japan-East Asia Network of Exchange for Students and Youths) Programme is an exchange project initiated by the Government of Japan to strengthen people-to-people connections among the youths in Japan and Southeast Asia. It is one of the four regional programs under Japanâ€™s Friendship Ties Programs. First launched in 2007, the JENESYS Programme invites young people from the Philippines and ASEAN member countries to participate in themed exchanges in Japan. For a period of ten (10) days, participants will take part in cultural activities, site visits, and workshops that focus on a specific topic which aim to promote appreciation of Japanâ€™s culture and society.  The programme is open to high school students, university students or young professionals, depending on the theme of the exchange.
The JENESYS Programme is organized by the Government of Japan in cooperation with partner organization and agencies in the Philippines.  

Part of the JENESYS Program is the Home Stay Program that is very valuable because the Japanese students experience the lives of local people and deepen mutual understanding between Japan and the Philippines family culture and traditions. 

The NYC is looking for three (3) host families that can accommodate the Japanese students on February 28- March 1, 2025. Each host family will be paired or match with two-three Japanese students.

    Qualifications of Host Families:

In order to make the JENESYS home stay program meaningful, JICE applies the following rules to match student with respective host family.
â€¢ One of the members of the host family should be a former JENESYS or other NYC International Programs beneficiary;
â€¢ The host family should be consist of at least two (2) adult members (One of the adult members must be the same gender as the Japanese students);
â€¢ One of the adult members of the host family of the same gender must stay home in the night of the homestay program;
â€¢ Two or more of the host family members of the host family must accompany and stay at the house during the homestay program;
â€¢ The host family member should have at least student(s) of the same gender and age or younger than the Japanese students;
â€¢ Can provide transportation service to and from designated homestay matching and closing ceremony venue (NYC Central Office);
â€¢ Can communicate with JICE Coordinator and NYC staff in case of emergency; and 
â€¢ Will allow the JICE Coordinator to visit the Japanese students during the home stay program.

Requirements:

â€¢ JENESYS Home Stay Program Application Form (You may access the form through this link: http://bit.ly/4jrniM0 )
â€¢ Scanned copy of passport or any valid ID of the Head of the Host Family member. 
â€¢ Attendance of the Host Families to the Home Stay Matching, which is scheduled on February 28, 2025 and on Closing Ceremony on March 1, 2025 at the NYC Central Office. 

Expenses:
To showcase the Filipino family hospitality culture, NYC is requesting all host families to provide and sponsor the Japanese students during the home stay program with the following but not limited to food, accommodation, transportation, tokens and tour to historical/ tourist spots near their area. 

JICE will be providing a minimal amount of â€œhome stay allowanceâ€ for the lunch and dinner of the Japanese students. 
During health emergency, it is understood that JICE will cover the hospitalization of the Japanese students. 
Hosting of JENESYS Japanese students/ delegates will be counted as 40 hour rendered volunteer service of former beneficiaries of JENESYS and other NYC international exchange programs.

â€¢ Do not submit multiple application forms. Please review your entries before submitting the form.

Deadline for submission is on February 10, 2025, at 11:59 pm.

#AreaNews | DRIVING ECONOMIC AWARENESS IN CORDILLERA THROUGH PSA-CAR ONLINE FORUM: INFLATION INSIGHTS REVEALED

The National Youth Commission (NYC)- Baguio Area Office recently attended an online forum hosted by the Philippine Statistics Authority- Cordillera Administrative Region (PSA-CAR) that sheds light on the region's inflation trends, offering stakeholders a comprehensive analysis of economic shifts. The region's inflation rate rose to 3.3%, reflecting an uptick from the previous monthâ€™s 2.4%, with an annual average inflation of 4.3%.

The rise was largely driven by the Food and Non-Alcoholic Beverages group, which surged to 5.0%, contributing 79.3% to the overall inflation rate. Significant increases were also observed in personal care, alcoholic beverages, and recreation commodities. Meanwhile, slower inflation rates were noted in housing, health, and clothing sectors, with some groups maintaining stability.

Food inflation, a major component of the region's economy, climbed to 5.0%, fueled by soaring prices in vegetables, seafood, and meat. Despite these pressures, reductions in rice, corn, and dairy inflation provided some relief.

Across provinces, Mountain Province recorded the highest inflation, while Apayao achieved the lowest, highlighting the diverse economic dynamics within the region. The data presented the need for continued collaboration to address inflationâ€™s impact on households, particularly for vulnerable groups.

#AreaNews | NYC Baguio Debunks Vaping Myths at â€œBreathe the Truthâ€ Webinar

The National Youth Commission- Baguio Area Office, represented by Dr. Sheridan Athena Gajete, participated as a panelist in the â€œBreathe the Truth: Redressing Misconceptions on Vaping and Smokingâ€ webinar organized by Transcend Philippines and Benguet Smoke-Free Philippines. Ms. Maria Cecilia Agpaoa, Head of Transcend Philippines, delivered the welcome message. The webinar brought together various schools and universities in the Cordillera Administrative Region (CAR).

During the panel discussion, Dr. Gajete addressed common misconceptions about e-cigarettes, emphasizing that despite being marketed as a safer alternative, they pose significant health risks. She debunked myths such as e-cigarettes being less addictive than tobacco cigarettes, nicotine-free e-cigarettes being harmless, vaping aiding smoking cessation and vaping being safer for loved ones. She also highlighted that e-cigarettes contain harmful chemicals, can be highly addictive, and may increase the likelihood of starting to smoke traditional cigarettes. She concluded her presentation with the powerful message: "Breathe air, do not smoke cigarettes and vape."

Testimonials by former residents of Regional Rehabilitation Centers for Youth were also featured. #AreaNews | RJJWC and RCAT-VAWC Conduct Year-End Program Review and Evaluation Workshop in Baguio City

The Regional Juvenile Justice and Welfare Committee (RJJWC) and the Regional Committee Against Trafficking and Violence Against Women and Children (RCAT-VAWC) recently conducted a year-end Program Review and Evaluation Workshop (PREW) in Baguio City. Attended by RJJWC and RCAT-VAWC members and representatives, the workshop focused on assessing the progress of both committees in fulfilling their mandates under the Juvenile Justice and Welfare Act of 2006 (RA 9344) and related laws. 

Key highlights included a presentation on "Understanding the Balance Approach Framework on Restorative Justice in relation to handling Children in Conflict with the Law (CICL)" by Elita B. Herrero, RJJWC Honorary Member, and a presentation on "Understanding Child Development and Dynamics of Abuse and Emotional Deprivation on Children" by Jewel Minlorry Llanillo-Villanueva, Assistant Head of SLU Sunflower Child and Youth Wellness Center. Participants presented their agency initiatives, contributions, and identified challenges and gaps encountered in implementing RA 9344 and related laws. The workshop concluded with participants formulating commitments and targets for 2025 in relation to RA 9344 as amended by Republic Act No. 11690, laws on trafficking, Violence Against Women and Children (VAWC), and Online Sexual Exploitation of Children (OSEC). 

The workshop provided a valuable platform for RJJWC and RCAT-VAWC members to review their accomplishments, identify areas for improvement, and strengthen their commitment to protecting the rights and welfare of children.

The National Youth Commission successfully conducted a three-day post-program evaluation and debriefing session for Batch Hiraya of the 48th Ship for Southeast Asian and Japanese Youth Program (SSEAYP) at Oracle Hotel, Quezon City.

LOOK | RIACYD XI Convenes in FY 2024 2nd Semester Meeting: Addressing Youth Concerns and Strengthening Partnerships

The Regional Inter-Agency Committee on Youth Development (RIACYD) XI, led by the Office of Commissioner representing Mindanao ASec. Bianca Patrice Go and NYC Region XI and BARMM Cluster, recently convened its second semester meeting to discuss pressing youth issues and explore innovative solutions. The meeting brought together key stakeholders from various government agencies, non-government organizations, and youth representatives to collectively address the challenges faced by the regionâ€™s youth.

A comprehensive Regional Youth Situationer was presented, highlighting the current state of youth development in Davao Region. This analysis provided valuable insights into the diverse needs and aspirations of young people, particularly in terms of education, employment, health, and social participation.

To effectively address these concerns, the meeting delved into a range of sectoral issues, including youth unemployment and underemployment, youth mental health, youth participation in governance.

To further strengthen youth development at the local level, the meeting encouraged LGUs to establish Local Youth Development Councils (LYDCs). By adopting a LYDC Resolution, LGUs can institutionalize youth participation and ensure that the voices of young people are heard and considered in local policy-making.

The RIACYD XI meeting concluded with a commitment to foster inclusive youth development in Davao Region.

LOOK | CPD AND NYC SOLIDIFY PARTNERSHIP THROUGH A MOA TO ADDRESS YOUTH ISSUES

The Commission on Population and Development (CPD) and the National Youth Commission (NYC) today signed a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) to strengthen their partnership and collaborate on initiatives aimed at empowering Filipino youth, particularly in addressing the issue of adolescent pregnancy.

The MOA signing ceremony was graced by NYC Chairperson and CEO USec. Jeff Ortega and CPD USec. Lisa Grace Bersales. Also present were Commissioner representing Luzon, Atty. Reena Vivienne Pineda, Commissioner representing Mindanao and PYDP Committee on Health Chairperson ASec. Bianca Patrice Go, and CPD Deputy Executive Director Lolito Tacardon.

Through this partnership, CPD and NYC will work together to enhance the capacities of youth leaders by equipping them with necessary skills and knowledge to develop effective youth-focused programs and initiatives; foster greater youth involvement in decision-making processes and policy development; and address adolescent pregnancy by collaborating on programs that promote sexual and reproductive health education, family planning, and gender equality.

In a message, USec. Ortega expressed his support for the partnership, emphasizing the potential of collaboration between CPD and NYC to positively impact the lives of young Filipinos, including those in women's correctional facilities and juvenile delinquents.

#AreaNews | Cultivating Future Leaders in Agriculture through PUNLA: National Agri Youth Summit 2024

The National Agri Youth Summit 2024 brought together young leaders, agriculture experts, and advocates to discuss the pivotal role of youth in the future of Philippine agriculture. The summit, held in Legazpi City, was an inspiring platform for youth to explore opportunities in agriculture and engage with experts such as Ms. Nydia P. Delfin, the NYC Naga Area Officer, who represented Assistant Secretary Christine Joy Cari.

Panelists emphasized the importance of integrating agriculture into youth development, with Ms. Delfin highlighting the Philippine Youth Development Plan's commitment to fostering agricultural initiatives. Recommendations for youth involvement included connecting with local agriculture offices, engaging with 4H clubs, and leveraging vacant lots for farming projects.

Resource speakers such as Mr. John Bryan E. Lawas, Mr. Emil Benjamin Tapnio, and Ms. Bernadette De Los Santos shared valuable insights on sustainable farming, nutrition, and the importance of policy advocacy. The summit culminated in an Agri-Immersion, where participants experienced firsthand the practices of beekeeping, cacao, and pili processing at the Albay Farmers Bounty Village.

The event, led by the Albay Young Farmers Organization, highlighted the significance of youth participation in agriculture, aiming to bridge knowledge gaps and inspire sustainable practices for the future of Philippine farming.

READ | NYC ADOPTS PHILIPPINE YOUTH STATEMENT ON CLIMATE CHANGE 2024, YOUTH GROUPS CALL FOR STRONG PH POSITION ON CLIMATE FINANCE AT COP29

Filipino youth are making their voices heard in the fight against climate change. 

At the Conference of Parties to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (COP29), a network of youth organizations, trade unions, and Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) councils submitted the Philippine Youth Statement on Climate Change 2024 to the Philippine delegation on Thursday, Nov. 14. 

The Youth Statement, which its proponents say is â€œthe most comprehensive expression of the Filipino youthâ€™s collective voice on climate,â€ urged the Philippine government to take a stronger stance in securing climate financing in order for the Philippines to transition away from fossil fuels, implement climate adaptation and resilience strategies, and establish better energy, transport, and waste management systems that create more jobs and bring development to the regions. 

â€œThis year alone, we experienced the worst heat wave ever, the first time that six storms hit the Philippines within a month, and the worst floods ever in the Bicol region. We are already reaching the limits of our resilience. This will be the future of the youth if no urgent action on the climate crisis is taken,â€ said Engr. Joshua Miguel Lopez, Chairperson of Reboot Philippines, a youth-led environmental group. 

Written by 210 youths from 58 organizations and trade unions, 44 Sangguniang Kabataan councils nationwide, with inputs from public consultations, the Philippine Youth Statement on Climate Change 2024 emphasizes: 
-transitioning to 75% renewable energy generation by 2030 and 100% by 2040 in line with the 1.5Â°C Paris goal;
-passing the Climate Accountability (CLIMA) Bill to ensure that climate polluters contribute financially to climate action;
-integrating climate change, energy transition, and disaster risk reduction education in the national education curriculum across all levels;
-developing well-designed, affordable, efficient, and modern mass public transportation systems;
-transitioning to a circular economy;
-prioritizing the creation of green and blue jobs; and
-institutionalizing youth advisory councils within local and national government

â€œThis statement displays the Filipino youthâ€™s steadfast commitment to a just and equitable future. It stresses the importance of moving beyond mere resilience, promoting the establishment of pro-people and pro-planet policies that tackle the root causes of climate change to safeguard the most vulnerable and heavily affected groups, including children, youth, women, students, fisherfolk, farmers, workers, and coastal populations,â€ said Chinito Casicas, Director of the Philippinesâ€™ Local Conference of Youth (LCOY) 2024.

The National Youth Commission (NYC) approved a resolution dated 13 November 2024 endorsing the Youth Statement to the Department of Environment and Natural Resources (DENR) and the Climate Change Commission (CCC). Representatives of Y4JT present at COP29 in Baku handed over the statement to DENR Undersecretary for Finance, Information Systems, and Climate Change Analiza Rebuelta-Teh.

â€œThe Youth Statement reflects the unified voice of the Filipino youth, demonstrating the urgent demands on climate adaptation, mitigation, finance, loss and damage, renewable energy, and just transition. The NYC acknowledges the critical role of youth in shaping climate policies and recognizes the Youth Statement as a vital advocacy for climate action, aligning with national and international commitments, including the Paris Agreement and the Philippine Climate Change Act,â€ said NYCâ€™s former Assistant Secretary Christine Joy Cari, who served as the Commissioner Representing Visayas and the PYDP Committee Chairperson on Environment.

The Philippine Youth Statement on Climate Change 2024 was written through a months-long series of consultations and workshops culminating in the Local Conference of Youth (LCOY) 2024, held on Oct. 25-27, 2024, at the National Irrigation Administration in Quezon City. LCOY 2024 was organized by the Youth for Just Transition (Y4JT) Network and supported by the National Youth Commission.

#AreaNews | NCR and MIMAROPA Successfully Hold Second Semester Regional Inter-Agency Committee on Youth Development (RIACYD) Meeting
 
The Regional Inter-Agency Committee on Youth Development Plan (RIACYD) for the National Capital Region (NCR) and MIMAROPA successfully held its second semester meeting on October 8 and 10, 2024, via Zoom. The primary purpose of the RIACYD is to review, prioritize, and advocate for regional youth development policies and programs at the national level, seeking funding and implementation. 

The committee also supports and collaborates with the National Youth Commission (NYC) and the Regional PYDP Task Force in executing these programs and projects. Additionally, the RIACYD provides updates and advice to NYC on youth-related policies, programs, and initiatives.

The meeting was attended by representatives from regional government agencies, SK Pederasyon Presidents, YORP members, LYDOs, and youth organizations from the major cities of NCR and the provinces of Oriental Mindoro, Occidental Mindoro, Marinduque, Romblon, and Palawan. Mr. Armando Angeles, Jr., Head of the NCR-MIMAROPA Cluster, presided over the gathering.

Mr. Ceajee F. Rosana, representing Atty. Reena Vivienne Pineda of the Office of the Commissioner Representing Luzon (OCRL), delivered the opening remarks. He expressed delight at the strong turnout and predicted productive discussions. Mr. Rosana commended the dedication and significant contributions of LYDOs, SKs, and LYDC members to youth development within their communities.

The meeting agenda included key topics such as updates on NYC SK policies and guidelines, progress reports from RIACYD members, and discussions on the mandatory bookkeeping training for SK Treasurers. These topics were thoroughly discussed during the meeting.

#AreaNews | NYC Baguio and NYC Pampanga Participate in Capacity Building for the Presidential Award for Child-Friendly Municipalities and Cities (PACFMC)

In support of the Presidential Award for Child-Friendly Municipalities and Cities (PACFMC), initiated by the Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) through Executive Order No. 184, NYC representatives, Ms. Sheridan Athena Gajete and Ms. Rose Chell Anne Rojas, took part in a five-day capacity building training.

This initiative, conducted by the Council for the Welfare of Children (CWC), aims to strengthen the capabilities of local government units (LGUs) in promoting child-friendly governance. The program focuses on ensuring children's rights to survival, development, protection, and participation are prioritized in LGU policies and programs.

Regional Director Dr. Maria Catbagen-Aplaten of DSWD CAR delivered the opening message, emphasizing the critical role of child-friendly LGUs. Ms. Gajete, on behalf of NYC, delivered the solidarity message, underscoring the Commission's commitment to this cause.

The training involved representatives from DSWD, CWC, PNP, DOLE, NEDA, DILG, and NYC, with Ms. April Obtinario serving as the trainer and facilitator. Key sessions covered essential areas such as the PACFMC evaluation process, service delivery in health, education, and social protection, and community participation.

As PACFMC validation resumes in 2025 following a four-year hiatus due to the COVID-19 pandemic, NYC remains dedicated to promoting programs that ensure the well-being and rights of children across municipalities and cities.

#AreaNews | Nurturing Resilience: Mastering Mental Well-Being

In celebration of Mental Health Awareness Month, the National Federation of Junior Philippine Institute of Accountants (NFJPIA) R1CAR, in collaboration with local chapters and various youth organizations, hosted an enlightening webinar titled "Anchoring the Mind: Mastering Inner Turbulence." This event provided a crucial platform for participants from universities such as MMSU, UNP, Lorma Colleges, BSU, and UC, as well as Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) members and youth leaders, to explore strategies for managing mental well-being.

The webinar featured Mrs. Kristina Camille Pacris-Quitevis from the University of Northern Philippines, who addressed a discussion on "Taming Your Own Giant." The National Youth Commission Baguio Area Office was also invited to be the second speaker, represented by Dr. Sheridan Athena Y. Gajete who discussed "Mind Your Mind: Hushing the Raging Sea Within."

During her presentation, she emphasized the unpredictable nature of life, likening emotional turmoil to the agitated surface of the ocean. Challenges and setbacks can create anxiety and overwhelm, yet the potential for inner calm lies beneath this turbulence. By embracing mindfulness practices, individuals can cultivate emotional stability and tranquility. Techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, and mindful observation anchor the mind, allowing thoughts and emotions to pass without judgment while fostering a sense of peace.

The webinar raised awareness about mental health and equipped participants with valuable insights and practices to navigate the complexities of their emotions, encouraging resilience and well-being among the youth.

#AreaNews | Provincial Micro, Small and Medium Enterprise Development
Council (PMSMEDC) Meeting Elevates Local Enterprises for Sustainable Growth

The Provincial Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprise Development Council (PMSMEDC) convened for its third-quarter meeting at the DTI Camarines Sur Provincial Office in Naga City, gathering key stakeholders dedicated to advancing MSME development. Attendees included representatives from essential government agencies such as the Department of Agriculture (DA), Department of Interior and Local Government (DILG), Department of Science and Technology (DOST), and National Youth Commission (NYC), as well as private sector partners from the Metro Naga Chamber of Commerce and Industry (MNCCI) and the Camarines Sur Chamber of Commerce and Industry (CSCCI).

The meeting commenced with updates from the secretariat, which reviewed the progress of previously agreed actions, allowing the council to evaluate ongoing projects and identify areas needing further focus. An orientation on the PMSMEDC's mandate informed new members about its mission to foster the growth of MSMEs in the province, emphasizing policy development, capacity building, and the provision of essential support services.

Discussions highlighted the challenges facing MSMEs, including access to financing, market linkages, and regulatory compliance. The council committed to collaborative efforts with government and private sector partners to address these concerns. New initiatives to support MSMEs were proposed, focusing on programs benefiting women and youth entrepreneurs.

Each participating agency presented its MSME-related initiatives, showcasing opportunities like DOST's scholarship grants, youth entrepreneurship programs, and the DA's Young Farmers Challenge, all designed to uplift emerging business leaders in the province.

The meeting concluded with stakeholders reaffirming their dedication to working together to support MSME development in Camarines Sur. Specific action points were established, with timelines for addressing the challenges discussed.

#AreaNews | NYC Naga Area Office Empowers SK Youth Through â€œABYIP 101: A Step-by-Step Guide to Developing the Annual Barangay Youth Investment Planâ€

The National Youth Commission (NYC) â€“ Naga Area Office recently hosted an informative webinar aimed at equipping Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) officials with the tools necessary to prepare and secure approval for their Annual Barangay Youth Investment Plan (ABYIP). Opening the session, Ms. Nydia P. Delfin, Area Officer of NYC Naga, highlighted the critical role of budget creation in fostering youth development, stressing that a well-structured ABYIP can address the diverse needs of young people.

Keynote speaker SK Chairman Angel Joy Gorospe of Barangay IV, Daet, Camarines Norte, shared her insights on crafting an effective ABYIP. She described the plan as a strategic roadmap for youth development, emphasizing the necessity of setting clear, realistic goals through the SMART frameworkâ€”Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound.

Expert panelists, including Mr. Francisco Mendoza, City Budget Officer of Naga, Mr. Ronalo Barbacerna, Youth Development Officer of Tabaco City, and Mr. James Nathaniel Refugio, Planning Officer IV of Naga City, provided guidance on local planning and budgeting. A key highlight of the webinar was the discussion on selecting priority projects. Participants were urged to promote sustainable development and align their initiatives with local and national youth development goals, focusing on immediate concerns and long-term empowerment.

In an interactive open forum, SK officials voiced concerns about budget conflicts, planning challenges, and transparency in fund allocation. Panelists offered strategies to enhance collaboration between SK officials and barangay councils, improve transparency in ABYIP implementation, and resolve budget conflicts while ensuring active stakeholder involvement.

#AreaNews | Strengthening Youth Development: Albay's Regional Inter-Agency Committee Meeting

The Regional Inter-Agency Committee on Youth Development convened at the Governorâ€™s Blue Conference Room in Legazpi City to discuss vital updates and strategies for the Philippine Youth Development Plan (PYDP) 2023-2028. The meeting emphasized the importance of the Monitoring, Evaluation, Accountability, and Learning (MEAL) system, with the Secretariat tasked to remind member agencies of their commitments to unify efforts for effective youth development programs.

Recognizing the current gap in regional youth data, the committee agreed to focus on consolidating the Regional Youth Development Index (YDI). The Secretariat will request member agencies to provide relevant data related to the ten centers of youth participation, ensuring comprehensive analysis and action.

Atty. Maria Juana S. Valeza, the COMELEC Regional Director, confirmed the postponement of the SK Special Election, urging the DILG to develop a mechanism for councils lacking quorum. The committee is also set to follow up on guidelines for filling vacant SK positions and discuss the Seal of Good Local Governance (SGLG) for Barangays in future meetings, identifying activities to enhance the functionality of Local Youth Development Councils (LYDC) and local plans.

The committee addressed pressing sectoral issues, such as the rise in HIV/AIDS cases among youth. The SKPF President and Provincial Youth Development Officers were encouraged to reactivate their Local Aids Councils, with the Department of Health providing materials for awareness campaigns. The National Nutrition Council will also include SK participation in initiatives to improve youth nutrition, particularly for ages 15-17.

Furthermore, the Secretariat gathered education-related data from CHED, DepEd, and TESDA to align with the Philippine Youth Development Index indicators. As part of the ongoing efforts to support youth development across the region, a permanent focal person will be designated from each member agency for consistent coordination.

The meeting concluded with plans for future gatherings, including presentations on Indigenous Peoples' Youth Organizations and discussions on youth engagement in agricultural programs. By fostering collaboration among agencies and focusing on pressing youth issues, the committee aims to enhance the overall development and empowerment of the youth in the region.

#AreaNews | Equipping Local Youth Leaders for Financial Excellence in Region IX

The National Youth Commission (NYC) has conducted a Training of Trainers (TOT) program focused on the Simplified Financial Transactions Module for Local Youth Development Officers (LYDO) in Region IX. This initiative aims to equip participants with the essential skills and knowledge to effectively train others in managing government financial transactions. The training is aligned with the Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) Reform Act, emphasizing the importance of youth involvement in local governance, particularly in financial management and accountability.

Through collaboration with key institutions, including the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) and the Development Academy of the Philippines (DAP), NYC has developed comprehensive training programs. The Simplified Financial Transactions Module is designed to provide SK officials with fundamental knowledge and skills for responsible public fund management, promoting financial integrity and transparency.

By enhancing the financial management capacities of SK officials, the NYC empowers them to fulfill their roles as public servants, effectively managing government resources and contributing to local development. The TOT program ensures a network of competent trainers capable of cascading the module nationwide, preparing SK officials to uphold integrity, transparency, and accountability in their financial responsibilities.

The meeting also highlighted the need for policies and programs that support youth-led health initiatives nationwide. #AreaNews | Connecting Futures: PSYIC 2024 Celebrates Youth Impact and Innovation in Bohol

The Philippines Sandugo Youth Impact Convergence (PSYIC) 2024, hosted by Youthlead Philippines and the Provincial Government of Bohol, was held in the heart of the UNESCO Global Geopark Regenerative Island. This landmark event, synchronized with the "Sandugo sa Bohol" festival, brought together a diverse group of youth development stakeholders from across the nation to share, connect, and learn from best practices in youth impact.

The convergence, inspired by the traditional Sandugo ceremony, aimed to forge stronger bonds and collaborations among youth leaders and stakeholders. The event saw participation from over 500 individuals, including Local Youth Development Officers, Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) officials, higher education institution student leaders, and community-based organizations.

Key presentations included Mr. Neil Andrew Manjares from NEDA 7, who detailed the alignment of the Philippine Development Plan (PDP) 2023-2028 with national and local youth programs. Ms. Cherry Mae Malate from NYC Cebu elaborated on the Philippine Youth Development Plan (PYDP) 2023-2028, emphasizing its alignment with national campaigns and its foundation in the Centers of Youth Participation.

Additional insights were shared by Ms. Reina Quilas from DILG Bohol, who discussed the SGLG Assessment and its impact on local youth development compliance. Mr. James Patrick Santos provided a historical perspective on the Oro Youth Code, highlighting its role in shaping youth programs before the SK Reform Law. Mr. Jovic Rapanan from Catbalogan City presented successful practices from the Katigtirok program, showcasing its impact on local youth engagement.

Mr. Metodio Maraguinot Jr., PYDO of Bohol and founder of YouthLead Philippines, concluded the event with a powerful discussion on effective and inclusive youth development. His message emphasized the need for transformational leadership and the importance of empowering youth to realize their potential.

The PSYIC 2024 not only celebrated the rich cultural heritage of Bohol but also reinforced the commitment to creating impactful and sustainable youth development initiatives across the Philippines.

#AreaNews | Angeles City Youth Development Alliance Enhances Capacity with OCAT Workshop

The Angeles City Youth Development Alliance (ACYDA), in collaboration with the Opportunity 2.0 Program, recently conducted an impactful orientation and workshop on the Organizational Capacity Assessment Tool (OCAT). This initiative is part of a broader USAID-supported effort aimed at improving the education, employment, and livelihood outcomes for out-of-school youth across 15 cities in the Philippines, including Angeles City.

The two-day event began with a warm welcome from Dr. Frankie Villanueva, ACYDA Chairperson, who set the stage for the day's proceedings. Mr. Jhon Hayden Bale, Youth Technical Lead, outlined the workshop's objectives and provided a detailed walkthrough of the planned activities. The Monitoring, Evaluation, and Learning (MEL) team then reviewed performance indicators related to the OCAT, focusing on key dimensions such as Governance, Strategic Direction, Planning & Service Delivery, Resource Mobilization, Networking & Advocacy, and Sustainability.

Following lunch, Ms. Jovelle Santiago, MEL Officer of Opportunity 2.0, discussed the YDA Report Card, highlighted specific OCAT indicators, and led a hands-on workshop to analyze and improve the allianceâ€™s functionality. The day concluded with a synthesis by Mr. Loven Ramos, Youth Development Coordinator of Opportunity 2.0, and a photo opportunity.

On the second day, Mr. Ramos opened with a review of objectives and a detailed walkthrough of the dayâ€™s activities. Ms. Santiago presented the full OCAT rating, providing valuable insights on planning and target setting. The participants were divided into groups to review and enhance their OCAT Action Plans, with each group presenting their findings and contributing to a collective synthesis. The workshop also included a review of the OSY FGD results and plans, the CCDA Annual Development Plan, and the CCYDA Annual Development Plan.

The workshop concluded with Mr. Ramos summarizing the day's achievements and another photo opportunity, marking a successful step towards enhancing the capacity and effectiveness of the Angeles City Youth Development Alliance.

#AreaNews | Empowering Tomorrow's Leaders: NYC Baguio Hosts Youth Development Training in Bacnotan

National Youth Commission (NYC) Baguio Area Office successfully conducted a one-day Youth Development through Leadership Training on August 30, 2024, in Bacnotan, La Union. The training brought together sixty participants from Local Youth Development Offices (LYDOs), Sangguniang Kabataan (SK) Federation members, partner agencies, and Local Youth Development Councils (LYDCs) from nine barangays within the municipality.

The event, hosted by Mr. Marc Jandel Buccat, LYDO of Bacnotan, opened with a welcoming address from Vice Mayor Francisco Fontanilla. Dr. Sheridan Athena Y. Gajete led discussions on leadership concepts, commitment setting, advocacy building, and the roles and functions of SKs, LYDCs, and LYDOs.

Representatives from Landbank and the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) offered valuable insights into the Landbank web portal and tax deductions, further supporting the participants' understanding of financial and administrative aspects.

The training session aimed to equip the youth with practical skills and knowledge necessary for effective leadership and community engagement. By fostering a collaborative learning environment, the event reinforced the importance of proactive and responsible leadership in driving positive change.
